question: What is Docker and what is it used for?
options: Docker is a platform for building, distributing, and running containerized applications. It makes it easy to package applications and deploy them to different environments.
Docker is a programming language for web applications

question: What is the difference between a Docker Image and a Docker Container?
options: A Docker Image is an immutable file containing a description of everything needed to run an application. A (Docker) Container is an instance of that image that runs as its own isolated process.

There is actually no practical difference between a Docker Image and a Docker Container, because both terms basically describe the same thing in different words. When someone says “image”, they already mean a container, and when they say “container”, it’s just another name for the same image.
A Container is a template for creating an Image, and the resulting image is generated only from a running container based on the current system state, similar to a virtual machine snapshot.

question: How does Docker Compose work?
options: Docker Compose allows you to define and manage multi-container applications (multiple instances run) using a YAML file, in which multiple instances are described; it is also possible to describe networking or disk management in more detail.

Docker Compose is a (GUI) tool for Docker where containers and their settings are usually configured by clicking in windows, without needing to write YAML or run commands in a terminal. It is used as a visual “container manager” where the user selects services, ports, etc.
Docker Compose is used only for testing

question: What is the difference between a Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml?
options: A Dockerfile defines how to build a container image, while docker-compose.yml is used to run and configure multiple containers at once.

A Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml are basically two different variants of the same configuration, because both files describe the same things and in practice have the same role. You could say it doesn’t matter which one you use, since both can build and run, and therefore they are commonly interchangeable without any loss of functionality.
docker-compose.yml defines the image build and also contains the entire build process including all instructions like RUN, COPY, and CMD, so there is no need to create a Dockerfile at all.

Docker does not support custom networks

In practice, role-based authorization is best handled by giving all users the same permissions so the API does not have to deal with deciding who can do what. Access is simplified by providing the same endpoints and capabilities to everyone, and any restrictions are not handled by roles but only by the client application "controlling" what it shows the user.
Authorization is handled on the client.

question: What is the difference between an API Proxy and an API Gateway?
options: An API Proxy only forwards requests; an API Gateway performs authentication, transformations, and monitoring.

An API Proxy and an API Gateway are exactly the same thing and it's just different terminology in different companies or tools. In both cases it's a "box in front of the API" that does the same job, so you cannot say that a gateway can do anything extra or that a proxy is simpler; the difference is only how it is named in documentation.
An API Proxy is a newer version of an API Gateway.
N/A

question: How can API abuse and bot attacks be mitigated?
options: Using rate limiting, CAPTCHA, IP whitelisting, and token-based authentication.

In reality, API abuse and bot attacks cannot be prevented at all, because once an endpoint is publicly available on the internet, anyone can send requests to it without limitation. Any limits or protections are supposedly just cosmetic and an attacker will always bypass them, so from an API design perspective it makes no sense to deal with rate limiting, CAPTCHA, or authentication as a defense against abuse.
Only by changing the URL.

question: How to implement a failover mechanism in an API?
options: Redirecting traffic to backup servers when the primary fails.

A failover mechanism is not needed because modern API servers are designed to practically never fail, and if something does go wrong, it's easier to ignore it and wait. The idea is that availability is not addressed with any redirection or redundancy because it would only add complexity; it is enough to run a single primary server.
By restarting the server.

question: What are webhooks and how do they work in an API?
options: Asynchronous HTTP callbacks that notify clients about events (e.g., GitHub webhooks).

Webhooks are basically just special types of API endpoints that do not differ from regular REST endpoints, only they have a different name in the documentation. The client calls them just like any other endpoint (for example via GET/POST whenever it wants), and it is not a callback or event notification—it's simply another normal URL in the API.
A security mechanism.

question: How do public, private, and partner APIs differ?
options: A public API is open to everyone, a private API is for internal use only, and a partner API is restricted to trusted third parties.

Public, private, and partner APIs are effectively the same and the difference is only in the name or a marketing label in documentation. From the perspective of authentication, rate limits, versioning, and legal terms, they are usually treated identically because it's still just one and the same technical service; the "public/private/partner" category does not really change how an API is designed or operated.
A private API is the most secure type.

A type of database.

options: SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF, man-in-the-middle attacks, brute force.

APIs have no security threats because they run on a server and the user has no direct access to it; if the UI is secured, the API is automatically secure and there is no need to deal with authentication or rate limiting.
Only DDoS attacks.

options: By using load balancing, failover strategies, and auto-scaling.

question: What is the Virtual DOM used for in React?
options: To diff changes between the previous and the new component tree and minimize the necessary updates to the real DOM.
To store the global state of the entire application.
To automatically cache all API responses in the browser so the same requests are not repeated.
To replace JavaScript when rendering components by having React first render the UI without JS and only then add interactivity purely using HTML.

question: Why is it important to use a stable key when rendering a list?
options: So React can correctly determine which items changed, moved, or were removed.
So you can use useState inside map(), because without a key React cannot assign hooks to individual list items and the application crashes.
So React can render the list only on the server (SSR) and on the client just "play back" the HTML without further work, because a stable key acts as an identifier for hydration.
So list items are automatically sorted by key (e.g., alphabetically or numerically) and React always displays them in a stable order regardless of the data.

question: What happens after calling the setter from useState?
options: React schedules a new render of the component with the updated state value.
The state is overwritten immediately and synchronously and React only swaps the variable value without ever re-rendering the component.
The component is always completely detached and reattached to the DOM (unmount/mount), so the entire "bootstrap" runs again, including losing all references and event handlers.
The change takes effect only after a page reload.

question: What does useEffect with an empty dependency array typically mean?
options: The effect runs after the first render of the component and the cleanup runs on unmount.
The effect runs after every render because an empty array means "depends on everything," i.e., on every variable the component uses.
The effect never runs because without dependencies React does not know when it should run it, so it skips it completely to be safe.
The effect runs only when props change, because an empty array is shorthand for "all props," while state is not counted as a dependency.

question: When does it make sense to use useMemo?
options: To memoize an expensive computation or value that should not be recomputed on every render when dependencies have not changed.
To replace useEffect for side effects, i.e., for API calls, working with timers, and registering listeners, because useMemo guarantees it runs only after the UI has rendered.
To directly mutate DOM nodes without re-rendering, for example to set classes and styles, because useMemo runs before the React render and can thus "prepare" the DOM.
To send an HTTP request on mount and automatically cache it, because useMemo performs the request only once and then always returns the same response.

question: What is useCallback used for?
options: To memoize a function reference between renders as long as its dependencies don’t change.
To asynchronously wait for the render to finish, so the called function runs only when React completes the DOM update and the layout is stable.
To store a mutable value without triggering a render (as an alternative to useRef), because useCallback returns an object that can be mutated freely and React won’t notice.
To define an error boundary in function components, because useCallback makes it possible to catch exceptions inside a subtree and return fallback UI.

question: When is it appropriate to use useRef?
options: To access a DOM element or store a mutable value that should not trigger a render.
To store global shared state for the entire application, because useRef is a "single instance" for the whole app and works similarly to a store.
To run a side effect after every render.
To replace props between parent and child, because a ref is passed automatically and guarantees that children will always see the latest value without re-rendering.

question: What is a controlled component in a form?
options: An input whose value is controlled by React state and changes via onChange.
An input that can never be edited by the user because it is "controlled" by React and all attempts to change it are blocked at the browser level.
An input that uses useRef exclusively instead of state and is therefore considered controlled.
An input that is rendered only on the server and never updates on the client, so it is "controlled" by the server during SSR.

question: What does lifting state up mean?
options: Moving shared state into the nearest common parent of the components.
Storing local state in localStorage so it is available even after a page refresh and can be "lifted" back into components on load.
Changing useState to useRef for performance, because "lifted" state no longer triggers renders and is cheaper to update.
Moving a component from the client to the server (converting it to a server component) so the state is computed on the backend rather than in the browser.

question: When is it appropriate to use React Context?
options: To pass values across many component levels without repeated prop drilling.
To replace all local state in an application, because Context is faster than useState and automatically solves render optimization as well.
To directly manipulate DOM nodes.
Only to store form inputs.

question: What does React.memo do?
options: Prevents unnecessary re-renders of a component if its props haven’t changed according to the comparison.
Memoizes the result of useEffect so the effect will not run again even if the dependency array changes and it will stay stored from the previous render.
Automatically caches all API requests made inside the component and returns the stored response on the next render without making a network call.
Converts a class component into a function component at runtime so hooks can be used in old classes without rewriting code.

question: Why is deriving state from props often problematic?
options: It can lead to duplicate sources of truth and inconsistent state.
Because React cannot pass props to child components, so once props change the children no longer receive them and the app breaks.
Because props cannot be read inside a component without using useEffect.
Because derived state is always faster than props, which makes React prefer derived state and ignore new props.

question: When is it better to use a functional state update setState(prev => ...)?
options: When the new value depends on the previous state value.
When we want to change the parent component's props, because a functional update has access to prev props and can overwrite them upward in the tree.
When using an uncontrolled input.
Only during server-side rendering.

question: What is a custom hook in React?
options: A custom function that uses other hooks for reusable stateful or side-effect logic.
A special class component for global state that is registered once in the app and then components can "extend" it similar to OOP.
A hook that can be used only in production because in development custom hooks are disabled due to StrictMode to avoid double invocation.
A tool for defining CSS styles—an equivalent of CSS-in-JS—where the custom hook returns classes and automatically injects styles into the page.

question: Why is cleanup important in useEffect?
options: So you can unregister listeners, cancel timeouts, or abort requests when dependencies change or on unmount.
So React can skip the next render, because cleanup "resets" the update queue and prevents unnecessary UI re-drawing.
So props are automatically serialized and reloaded on unmount/mount, which lets you preserve component state even without external storage.
So useEffect runs synchronously during render. Cleanup ensures the effect runs before React updates the DOM.

question: What will you typically see in React StrictMode during development?
options: Some lifecycles and effects may be intentionally invoked multiple times to detect impure logic.
The production build is automatically twice as fast because StrictMode enables internal React optimizations that otherwise wouldn’t be used.
React disables useEffect and useMemo because side effects are dangerous in StrictMode and React enforces a purely declarative render without hooks.
Every component must be written as a class, because StrictMode is meant for the old API and function components with hooks are not supported in it.

question: What can an error boundary catch?
options: Errors during rendering, in lifecycle methods, and in constructors of the component subtree.
Errors in asynchronous requests without additional handling, because an error boundary automatically wraps all Promises and converts their rejection into a render error.
All errors in event handlers automatically, because before running a handler React adds try/catch and forwards the exception to the nearest error boundary.
Errors in CSS and page layout.

question: What is React Suspense used for?
options: To declaratively show fallback UI while waiting for lazy-loaded parts or supported async resources.
To replace all error boundaries
To directly store data in localStorage so rendering can be "suspended" and resumed after restoring data from storage without additional logic.
To measure render performance in DevTools, because Suspense collects metrics about render duration and adjusts update priority based on them.

question: What is the stale closure problem?
options: A callback or effect works with stale variable values because it captured an older render.
A hook is called outside a React component, creating a "closed" context that can no longer be updated and React considers it a stale closure.
State is written directly into the DOM.
Props change too quickly on the server for the client to hydrate in time, creating a stale closure between the SSR and CSR versions of the component.

question: What happens if you change the key on the same component?
options: React will remount the component and recreate its local state.
React preserves state and the DOM unchanged, because key is only a hint for developers in DevTools and has no effect on reconciliation behavior.
React changes only the component’s CSS classes, because key is used as a prefix for generated className and therefore affects styling, not the lifecycle.
Changing the key has no effect.

question: How should you correctly update an array in state?
options: Immutably, by creating a new array—for example using map, filter, or the spread operator.
By directly mutating the original array and without calling the setter.
Only via useRef to avoid a render.
Always using JSON.stringify.

question: When is it appropriate to use useReducer instead of useState?
options: When state update logic is more complex or state consists of multiple related transitions.
When the component doesn’t need state at all.
Only for storing DOM references.
Only in class components.

question: What is the difference between state and ref?
options: Updating state triggers a render, while updating a ref typically does not.
A ref is just another name for props.

A ref is used only for server rendering.

question: What does props.children represent?
options: Content placed between the opening and closing tags of a component.
An automatically generated state of child components.
An internal list of all hooks in the component.
Only text content without JSX.

question: What is React Portal used for?
options: To render part of the UI into a different DOM node outside the current parent component hierarchy.
To create a global state store.
To dynamically load CSS modules.
To serialize props on the server.

question: What is a common problem with using an index as a key in a dynamic list?
options: Reordering items can cause incorrect association of state or DOM elements.
React then cannot render the list at all.
Using an index as a key always increases component security.
Using an index as a key is required to use useMemo.

question: Why is it important to have the correct dependency array for useEffect?
options: So the effect reacts to relevant changes and does not work with stale values.
So the hook can be called inside conditions.
So React can skip all subsequent renders.
The dependency array is only for typing in TypeScript.

question: When does an uncontrolled input make sense?
options: When you don’t want to keep the value in state on every keystroke and it’s enough to read it—for example via a ref—when submitting the form.
When you want to validate every keystroke via React state.
When you need to share an input across multiple pages.
Only for server components.

question: What is startTransition in React?
options: An API for marking less urgent updates that can be deferred in favor of a responsive UI.
A hook for directly changing the URL.
A mechanism for synchronous rendering without interruption.
A replacement for useEffect when fetching data.

question: What is useDeferredValue used for?
options: To defer propagating a rapidly changing value into a more expensive part of the UI.
To permanently store a value in localStorage.
To prevent a parent component from rendering.
To define a lazy-loaded component.

question: What is a hydration mismatch?

A state where the server returns more CSS files than the client.
A situation where useState returns undefined.

question: What is a synthetic event in React?
options: A wrapper around native browser events that provides a consistent API across browsers.
An event that works only on the server.
An event created only for tests in Jest.
A DOM node stored in useRef.

question: What does it mean to colocate state?
options: To keep state as close as possible to where it is actually used.
To move all state into a global context.

To always use only useReducer.

question: How does React preserve component state between renders?
options: Based on the component’s position in the tree and its identity, including its key.
Based on the names of variables inside the function.
Only based on the order of imports in the file.

question: When should you use useLayoutEffect instead of useEffect?

options: Multiple state updates can be merged into fewer renders even outside classic event handlers.

question: What is the recommended way to cancel a fetch request inside an effect?

question: What is React.lazy used for?

question: What is the benefit of render props compared to hardcoded logic inside a component?

question: Why can an inline object or function in props cause unnecessary re-renders?
options: Because a new reference is created on every render and a shallow compare evaluates it as a change.
Because React cannot serialize objects.

question: How does a Context Provider behave when the value object changes?
options: Context consumers may re-render if the identity of the provided value changes.

question: Why are side effects not suitable directly inside the render function?
options: Render should be pure and repeatable; side effects in it lead to inconsistent behavior.
Because the render function runs only once when the application starts.
Because side effects are allowed only in class components.

question: What is an optimistic UI update?
options: Temporarily reflecting the expected outcome in the UI before the server confirms it.
Rendering data only after all requests have fully completed.

question: What is the difference between props and state?
options: Props come from outside (from the parent), while state is internally managed component state.
Props and state change only via the DOM API.
State is immutable; props are mutable.
Props can be used only in class components.

question: What is the main advantage of composition via children or props compared to deep inheritance?
options: Better composition of responsibilities and more flexible reuse of UI and logic.
Lower memory usage in the browser under all circumstances.
Automatic handling of asynchronous requests.
A requirement to use fewer components.

question: What rule applies to calling hooks?
options: Hooks should always be called at the top level of a React function or a custom hook, not conditionally inside branches and loops.
Hooks can be called only inside useEffect.
Hooks must always be wrapped in try/catch.
Hooks can be used only in three imported files.

question: What is the main benefit of Vue reactivity?
options: It automatically tracks dependencies and re-renders only the parts of the UI that depend on the changed data.
It lets you write components completely without JavaScript, because reactivity means all logic is moved into the template and Vue evaluates it by itself without scripts.
It replaces the need for props and emits, because the reactive system automatically links parent and child state bidirectionally, so components do not need any explicit communication.
It works only during server-side rendering (SSR), because reactivity is a property of the server process; on the client the UI updates only on page reload.

question: What is the Composition API used for in Vue?
options: To organize reusable logic by responsibility using setup(), composables, and reactive primitives.
To replace templates with pure CSS: instead of a template, the component is "composed" from CSS selectors and Vue then automatically generates the HTML structure based on them.
To automatically generate the router from the folder structure (file-based routing), where the Composition API scans directories and creates all route records for you.
Only to define a global store for the whole application. Without the Composition API, it is not possible to share state between components, and it is basically a built-in replacement for Vuex/Pinia.

question: What is the difference between ref and reactive?
options: ref typically wraps a single value in an object with .value, while reactive creates a reactive proxy for an object.
ref works only for DOM nodes (similar to React) and is used exclusively to get an element from the template, while reactive is intended only for arrays and collections.
reactive is immutable (cannot be changed) because Vue requires pure functions, while ref is mutable and can be overwritten directly like a normal variable.
There is no difference between them: ref and reactive are just two names for the same mechanism and Vue always internally converts them into the same structure.

question: When is it appropriate to use computed instead of a method?
options: When you want a derived value with caching based on reactive dependencies.
When you want to run side effects after every render, such as writing to localStorage or sending analytics; computed is ideal for this because it is evaluated "automatically".
When you need to emit an event to the parent, because computed can return not only a value but also automatically call emit() on every dependency change.
When you want to mutate props, because computed behaves like a "safe wrapper" around props and lets you overwrite props directly without console warnings.

question: What is watch used for?
options: To react to changes of a specific reactive value or expression, typically for side effects.
To declare slots in a child component, because watch can track which slot is currently filled and automatically switches the component content accordingly.
To replace v-if in the template: watch hides or shows elements directly in the DOM without needing directives for conditional rendering.
To directly change the template DOM without rendering—as a low-level DOM API layer that allows overwriting HTML inside a component without reactivity.

question: How is watchEffect different from watch?
options: watchEffect automatically tracks the dependencies used inside its callback, while watch has its watch source defined explicitly.
watchEffect works only in the Options API and is used instead of mounted/updated.
watch does not support cleanup (unregistering listeners, etc.), so it’s only suitable for simple logging; cleanup is supported only by watchEffect, which is designed specifically for it.
watchEffect is intended only for SSR, because on the client automatic dependency tracking would be too slow and Vue therefore disables it.

question: What does it mean that props in Vue are one-way data flow?
options: The parent passes values to the child component, but the child should not mutate them directly.
The child can freely change props and the parent won’t see it, because one-way means changes from the child are never propagated back to the parent and Vue intentionally ignores them.
Props can flow only between sibling components, because one-way data flow forbids direct communication between parent and child and requires sharing through a mediator.
Props can only be used for boolean values, because one-way data flow is implemented as an on/off switch and Vue does not support more complex types.

question: How does a child component typically communicate a change to the parent?
options: By emitting an event that the parent listens to and reacts to.
By directly mutating the parent's state without an event, because Vue automatically links all nested components and a child can safely overwrite the parent's variables.
By overwriting its own props, because props are two-way and the child can change them.
Only through a global store, because without Pinia/Vuex there is no official way to send information from child to parent.

question: What is v-model used for?
options: For two-way binding between a UI value and a change event and the component’s state.
To define slots for a child component, because v-model determines what "model" of slots the component accepts and how their content maps into the template.
To disable reactivity for a given input so that no watchers or computed properties run while typing; the value is stored only when the form is submitted.
To memoize computations in the template: v-model stores the last rendered value and prevents recomputation until the entire form changes.

question: What is the difference between v-if and v-show?
options: v-if adds or removes the element from the DOM, while v-show typically just toggles CSS display.
v-show removes the element from the DOM and v-if does not. v-if supposedly only sets visibility: hidden so the element can’t be clicked, but it stays in the DOM.
Both directives always do the same thing and it’s just syntactic sugar. Vue internally rewrites them to the same code and performance is identical.
v-if can be used only on form elements (input/select), because only those support conditional rendering. For other elements you must use v-show.

question: What are slots used for?
options: To pass renderable content into a component from the outside while keeping its wrapper structure.
To store local state between routes, because a slot represents a UI "stack" that Vue preserves and reinserts when returning to a page.
To replace props in every component—instead of passing data via props, Vue recommends passing everything via slots because they are more type-safe.
To define HTTP route handlers, i.e., to map URLs to components.

question: What are scoped slots?
options: Slots where the child provides data to the parent for rendering the slot content.
Slots that work only with scoped CSS: if a component doesn’t have the scoped attribute in its styles, scoped slots won’t render and Vue ignores them.
Slots available only in production, because in development they would transfer too much data and slow down hot reload—so Vue enables them only at build time.
A direct replacement for provide/inject, because scoped slots automatically inject values into all descendants without the need to pass props or context.

question: When should you use provide/inject?
options: To pass values across the component tree without prop drilling, typically for technical context or plugin APIs.
To store every form input in the application, because provide/inject is the recommended way for "global forms" and it automatically handles validation and reset.
To replace the router, because provide/inject can propagate the current URL into components and thus ensures navigation without Vue Router.
Only for asynchronous data from the backend, because inject automatically waits for a Promise and ensures the child gets data only after it has loaded.

question: What is a composable in Vue?
options: A function that encapsulates reusable logic built on the Composition API.
A special template directive for conditional rendering that "composes" multiple v-ifs into a single clear syntax and speeds up rendering.
A replacement for a CSS module: a composable defines scoped styles and Vue then automatically attaches it to the corresponding component based on the import.
A mandatory structure of every page in Vue Router (e.g., usePage()), without which the router will refuse to load the component and show an error.

question: What does onMounted do?
options: Registers a callback that runs after the component has been mounted into the DOM.
Runs cleanup on unmount: it is used to unregister listeners and cancel timers, because its name means "when the component is unmounted".
Adds a value to the computed cache so computed is evaluated only once after mount and never recalculated again until the route changes.
Works only on the server during SSR.

question: What is onUnmounted used for?
options: To clean up listeners, timers, or other side effects when the component is removed.
To initialize props before the first render, because it runs before mount and allows changing props so they match the component type.
To define emitted events: onUnmounted is used to register all emit names and Vue then validates events at runtime because of it.
To attach SSR middleware, i.e., to hook server-side hooks into a component so it can handle an HTTP request during unmount.

question: What is the purpose of key when rendering a list in Vue?
options: It helps the framework correctly identify items during list updates and preserve the right state.
It allows using v-if inside v-for, because without a key Vue cannot evaluate the condition for individual items and conditional rendering in a loop is forbidden.
It replaces the need for :class, because Vue automatically generates CSS classes for each item based on the key (e.g., item-1, item-2) and you can style them directly.
A key is needed only for server rendering, because on the client Vue does not recycle lists and always re-renders them completely, so item identification is not necessary.

question: Why is it a problem to use the index as a key in a dynamic list?
options: When order changes, it can lead to incorrect reuse of DOM or the items’ local state.
Vue then won’t render the list at all, because an index is just a number and Vue requires keys strictly as UUID strings; numeric keys are invalid.
Using the index as a key automatically disables reactivity: Vue considers items "static" and stops tracking changes until you change the entire array object.
An index key can be used only with slots, because indexes are internally reserved for slot props.

question: What is a computed setter?
options: The ability to define not only a getter but also write logic for a computed value.
A special lifecycle hook that runs on every computed re-evaluation and lets you "set" the evaluation order of computed properties in a component.
A mandatory parameter of watchEffect without which watchEffect doesn’t know what value to return; the computed setter determines where the watchEffect result should be stored.
A mechanism for SSR hydration where the computed setter fills values into the HTML on the first render and then "sets" them into component state on the client.

question: Why is it not recommended to perform side effects directly inside computed?
options: A computed property should be a deterministic derived value, not a place for side effects.
Because a computed property is evaluated only once after the app starts and never again; side effects would therefore not run reliably on subsequent data changes.
Because computed cannot work with props and only has access to local state.
Because computed works only in class components (similar to other frameworks) and in modern Vue with the Composition API computed is only an alias for a method.

question: What does nextTick do?
options: Waits for pending DOM updates to be flushed so you can work with the up-to-date rendered DOM.
Moves the component to the next route, because "tick" means a router step; nextTick is syntactic sugar for router.push().
Forces a full app reload and remounts the root component; it is used when Vue "gets stuck" and the UI needs to be restarted.
Replaces setTimeout because it always waits exactly 1 ms and then runs the callback; it is the recommended way to do short delays without blocking the UI.

question: What is the purpose of defineProps in <script setup>?
options: Declares the props accepted by the component in <script setup> syntax.
It is used to create a global store, because defineProps defines the application's "global properties" which then become available in all components without importing.
It is used only in the Options API where it replaces props: { ... }; it is not used in <script setup> because props are declared exclusively in the template there.
It adds scoped CSS to the component: Vue takes prop names from defineProps and generates unique CSS selectors from them so styles don’t leak.

question: What is defineEmits used for?
options: To declare and type the events that a component emits.
To directly change the parent component’s props, because emits are two-way and defineEmits allows the child component to overwrite parent values without explicit handlers.
To create a watcher on route params; defineEmits is part of Vue Router and is used so that a URL change automatically emits an event into the component.
To disable reactivity in a child component, because emitting events is expensive. defineEmits switches the component into a "static" mode.

question: What does <script setup> enable?
options: A more concise Composition API syntax with less boilerplate and direct availability of declarations in the template.
Rendering without a template and without JSX: <script setup> supposedly means the component is rendered automatically based on the script’s return value and you don’t write any HTML at all.
Automatic generation of unit tests during build, because <script setup> contains metadata from which Vue generates tests for props, emits, and slots.
Usage only on the server, because <script setup> is an SSR-only syntax mode and for security reasons it would not work in the browser.

question: When should you use shallowRef?
options: When you want to track reference changes but do not want deep reactivity of the nested object.
When you need a computed value with caching: shallowRef is an optimized version of computed that stores the last result and recalculates only when props change.
When you want to access a DOM element in the template, because shallowRef is automatically populated with the DOM node after render and it is the recommended approach instead of ref.
When you need a deep watcher for every nested key, because shallowRef is a "deep" ref and tracks changes in all nested objects without needing deep: true.

question: What is toRefs used for?
options: To convert a reactive object into individual refs while preserving reactivity.
toRefs removes all watchers from the component because it "breaks" a reactive object into values that are no longer tracked, thereby turning off change tracking.
To convert props into an immutable object: toRefs turns props into immutable refs and Vue then guarantees their .value cannot be overwritten even by accident.
To serialize router state: toRefs converts the current route to JSON and stores it in history so the state can be restored after refresh.

question: What is a common mistake when destructuring a reactive object?
options: Losing reactivity if you do not extract values via toRefs or another appropriate mechanism.
Immediately triggering all watchers twice.
Turning all properties into readonly.
It is not a mistake; reactivity is always preserved.

question: How does Vue typically handle form validation?
options: By combining reactive state, computed/watcher logic, and optionally a validation library.
Only via the HTML required attribute; nothing else is possible.
Validation is built into v-model automatically for all scenarios.
Only via provide/inject.

question: What is the main benefit of Vue Router?
options: It maps URLs to components and provides mechanisms such as nested routes, guards, and route params.
It replaces the need for props and emits.
It stores data in localStorage.
It is used exclusively for styling the page.

question: What is a navigation guard used for?
options: To control access or run logic before or during a route change.
To memoize computations in a component.
To directly mutate the DOM in a child component.
To add a CSS animation to the transition.

question: What is the Teleport component in Vue?
options: It allows rendering part of the content into a different place in the DOM outside the current component tree.
It moves the application between the client and the server.
It replaces both slots and props.
It is used to define a global store.

question: What is Suspense used for in Vue?
options: For working with async components or async setup logic and displaying fallback content while waiting.
To store test results in the store.
To disable SSR hydration.
To measure FPS in the application.

question: What is an async component?
options: A component that is loaded dynamically only when it is needed.
A component that can have only async methods.
A component without a template.
A component intended only for a Node.js backend.

question: Why is it important not to violate unidirectional data flow?
options: It simplifies debugging and reduces the risk of hidden side effects between parent and child components.
Because Vue would otherwise disable reactivity for the entire app.
Because props can be used only one way due to TypeScript.
Because emit events work only in SSR.

question: What is the main role of Pinia in the Vue ecosystem?
options: To provide a simple, type-friendly store for shared application state.
To replace Vue Router.
To convert templates into CSS modules.
To enable rendering without the DOM.

question: What does lazy loading routes mean in Vue Router?
options: That route components are loaded dynamically only when entering the given route.
That the router waits for CPU idle time before every navigation.
That all routes are rendered only on the server.
That route params are loaded from localStorage.

question: What is the difference between local state and a store?
options: Local state is limited to a specific component, while a store is used to share state across the application.
A store is just another name for props.
Local state cannot be used in Vue.
A store works only in the Options API.

question: What does computed typically do better than watch?
options: It represents a derived value without side effects and with internal caching.
It allows emitting an event to the parent component.
It replaces the need for route guards.
It is used to cancel pending requests.

question: What is the difference between v-bind and v-on?
options: v-bind passes values to attributes or props, while v-on attaches event listeners.
v-bind is for events and v-on is for props.
Both are used only for CSS classes.
Both directives work only in the Options API.

question: What is an advantage of scoped CSS in Vue single-file components?
options: It limits the impact of styles to a specific component without having to manually namespace all selectors.
It guarantees full isolation without any exceptions even for global styles.
It replaces the need for CSS variables.
It works only with v-show.

question: What is the main purpose of emit validation or typing?
options: To provide a clear contract about which events a component can emit and with what payload.
To allow a child component to directly change the route configuration.
To prevent a parent component from responding to events.
To disable props reactivity.

question: What is keep-alive used for?
options: To preserve a component instance when switching, typically for dynamic components or routes.
To permanently store data in browser storage.
To force a re-render after every click.
To disable transitions.

question: What is the main advantage of Single File Components?
options: They allow keeping a component’s template, logic, and styles together in a clearly structured file.
They are mandatory for every Vue application.
They can be used only without a bundler.
They replace the need for a router.

question: What is the purpose of performance optimization via splitting components?
options: It reduces the amount of reactive dependencies in one place and limits the scope of updates.
Vue then does not need to use the Virtual DOM at all.
It allows skipping all router guards.
It guarantees zero re-renders.

question: What is a hydration mismatch in Vue SSR?
options: A mismatch between server-rendered HTML and what the client expects during hydration.
Disabling the router when switching to the client.
A state where Pinia contains no store.
A syntax error in v-bind.

question: What is defineExpose used for?
options: To explicitly expose selected values or methods to the parent via a template ref.
To export all props into a global store.
To define route guards in a component.
To switch from the Composition API to the Options API.

question: Why can deep watch hurt performance?
options: Because it tracks changes in nested structures and can be expensive for large objects.
Because Vue does not support deep watch and will always crash.
Because deep watch works only for primitive types.
Because a watcher automatically disables computed.

question: What is an appropriate approach to fetching data in a component?
options: Run it in an appropriate lifecycle hook or a composable, and handle loading, error, and cleanup scenarios.
Call fetch directly in a template expression.
Always store the response in props.
Use only watchEffect, even if it complicates control over dependencies.

question: When is it appropriate to use readonly on a reactive value?
options: When you want to pass state further as read-only and prevent unintended mutations from other places.
When you want to remove a value from the reactive system.
When you need a deep watcher.
Only when using Vue Router.

question: What is the main role of the template compiler in Vue?
options: It compiles templates into render functions that the framework executes.
It stores component state in JSON files.
It automatically creates unit tests.
It handles HTTP requests on the backend.

question: What is the main characteristic of the Node.js runtime?
options: A JavaScript runtime built on V8, primarily for server-side and CLI applications with an event-driven I/O model.
A framework for building CSS layouts that adds a special "Node stylesheet engine" to the browser and lets you write grids and flexbox using JavaScript on the backend.
A document-oriented database where collections are stored as .js files and queries are written directly in the Node API without any database server.
A tool only for building frontend assets, similar to Webpack/Vite, whose sole purpose is bundling and minifying JS/CSS for production.

question: What is the event loop in Node.js?
options: A mechanism that processes callbacks and asynchronous tasks across the individual phases of execution.
A queue only for HTTP requests that Node uses instead of TCP sockets. All other types of I/O are processed synchronously outside the event loop.
A tool for JSON serialization that "spins" objects in memory so they can be safely converted to a string without cyclic references.
An API for creating worker threads.

question: Why is Node.js suitable for I/O-bound workloads?
options: Because non-blocking I/O allows handling many waiting operations without blocking the main thread.
Because every I/O operation automatically runs on its own processor (dedicated CPU core), so network and disk never compete for time with JavaScript.
Because JavaScript code is always faster than native C/C++ code, so network and disk operations are more performant when done in JS.
Because Node does not use network sockets and instead has a direct "channel" to the internet that does not block even with a large number of connections.

question: What is libuv used for in Node.js?
options: It provides the event loop, abstracts asynchronous I/O, and provides a thread pool for some operations.
It is a tool for transpiling TypeScript to JavaScript that runs before V8.
It is used to render HTML templates on the server and includes a built-in template engine. Express uses it as the default view renderer.
It is a package manager for npm packages and ensures installation of native module dependencies into the libuv_modules/ folder.

question: What is the main difference between CommonJS and ESM?
options: CommonJS uses require/module.exports, while ESM uses import/export and has a different module system and loading behavior.
CommonJS works only in the browser and ESM only on the server; therefore in Node, CommonJS is not used and everything is mandatory ESM.
ESM cannot work with async imports and must always load all modules synchronously at application start, which is why only CommonJS is used for lazy-loading.
CommonJS supports only CSS files and ESM only JavaScript; if you want to import JSON, you must always use CommonJS.

question: What does it mean that Node runs on a single-threaded JavaScript execution model?
options: JavaScript code runs in one main thread, although some I/O or internal operations may use a thread pool or the OS.
Node never uses any additional threads even for internal operations; everything including file reads and DNS always runs purely in a single JavaScript thread.
Each request automatically gets its own JavaScript thread, similar to traditional servers. Therefore Node is "single-threaded" only from the perspective of a single request.
Node can execute only synchronous functions; asynchronous code does not exist in Node and callbacks are just a syntactic trick for writing synchronous operations.

question: When can a synchronous API like fs.readFileSync hurt server performance?
options: When it blocks the event loop and prevents serving other requests while reading.
Never—synchronous APIs are always faster because they run "directly" and Node doesn’t use any overhead; that’s why it’s recommended to use them in every request handler.
Only when used in tests, because the test runner can measure time and synchronous calls would skew results.
Only on Windows, because on Linux Node automatically converts readFileSync into an asynchronous operation via libuv and the event loop is not blocked.

question: What are streams used for in Node.js?
options: To process data incrementally in chunks without having to hold everything in memory at once.
To store environment variables in a special configuration "stream" that Node automatically shares between processes in a cluster.
To define HTTP route handlers, because each Express endpoint is actually a stream and middleware are transform streams.
To replace the Promise API: streams are a more modern way to handle async and gradually replace async/await in Node core.

question: What is backpressure in streams?
options: A flow-control mechanism so a producer doesn’t generate data faster than a consumer can process it.
Automatic request retry on error: when a stream hits an error, backpressure rewinds it to the beginning and Node re-sends the same data.
A name for a memory leak in Buffers that happens when data is kept "at the back" of a stream for a long time; backpressure refers to this exact bug.
A feature available only for child processes, because only between processes do you need to regulate data flow; within a single process, streams do not support backpressure.

question: What is Buffer used for in Node.js?
options: To work with binary data outside of normal JavaScript strings and arrays.
To cache React components so Node doesn’t have to recompile JSX on every request; Buffer is intended as a "render buffer" for this.
To control the event loop, because Buffer is an internal event queue and you can manually add and remove tasks from it for better performance.
To define TypeScript generics: Node Buffer contains metadata about types and allows verifying at runtime that values match generic parameters.

question: What is the difference between process.nextTick() and setTimeout(..., 0)?
options: process.nextTick() runs sooner—before the event loop proceeds to the next phase—while the timeout waits for the timers phase.
Both APIs are completely identical in Node; they differ only by name and exist historically for compatibility, but scheduling is the same into the same queue.
setTimeout(..., 0) always runs before microtasks, so it is a reliable way to outrun Promise callbacks and run logic "as soon as possible".
process.nextTick() is only for child processes; in a normal application outside the child_process module it won’t run and Node ignores it.

question: What is the Promise microtask queue?
options: A queue of callbacks from Promise reactions that is processed before moving on to further event loop phases.
A queue only for HTTP responses where Node stores callbacks from res.end(); Promises are not actually scheduled into it at all.
A storage for environment variables, because microtasks need access to configuration; Node therefore stores them in a special "queue" in the Promise implementation.
A mechanism available only in the browser; in Node, Promise callbacks always run only in the next event loop iteration as normal macrotasks.

question: When does it make sense to use worker_threads?
options: For CPU-intensive work that you don’t want to block in the main JavaScript thread.
For normal file reading via fs.promises, because a worker thread is the fastest way to do I/O and without it all file operations are blocking.
To replace all Promise chains, because worker_threads automatically parallelize await and split every Promise across multiple threads.
Only for serving static assets, because worker_threads in Node are designed mainly for sending files (CSS/JS/images) faster than Express.

question: When is child_process more appropriate than a worker thread?
options: When you need a separate process, a different runtime, or to run external binaries.
When you want to share the JavaScript heap between threads, because a child process has access to the same memory and is therefore better for sharing large objects without copying.
For a simple object update in state, because child_process is lighter than a functional update in JS and solves concurrency automatically by running each update in a new process.
Only when using Express, because child_process is part of the Express API and is not used outside web apps (CLI, jobs).

question: What is cluster in Node.js?
options: A mechanism to run multiple processes to utilize multiple CPU cores for the same server workload.
A native database replication layer that automatically synchronizes data between multiple Node instances and guarantees ACID transactions.
A name for a group of Promise callbacks that Node groups into a "cluster" so it can evaluate them in parallel without waiting for the event loop.
A mandatory part of every Node application: if you don’t use cluster, Node will run only in "demo mode" and limit the number of concurrent connections.

question: What is the main advantage of the fs.promises API?
options: It allows convenient filesystem operations using Promises and async/await.
All operations become synchronous thanks to it, because a Promise in Node means "it will wait", so the event loop stops while working with files until completion.
It works only in a worker thread, because the Promise filesystem API requires a parallel thread and is disabled in the main thread.
It replaces the need for imports, because when you use fs.promises, Node automatically imports all core modules into the global scope.

question: How do you correctly catch an error in an async function?
options: Use try/catch around await or correctly handle the returned Promise.
Rely on Node to print all errors and safely continue; async errors supposedly never propagate and Node always just logs them to the console.
Use only console.log without any other logic, because printing the error automatically marks the Promise as "handled" and the app will no longer treat it as a problem.
Catch the error only at the package.json level by adding an errors: true field, which enables a global handler so local try/catch is not needed.

question: What is an unhandled promise rejection?
options: A Promise that is rejected and does not have an error handler attached in time.
A Promise that stays pending longer than 5 seconds; Node treats it as a rejection to prevent infinite waiting and marks it as unhandled.
Any throw inside a sync function, because Node internally converts it into a Promise rejection and if you don’t have await, it is considered unhandled.
A situation where await returns undefined; Node interprets that as a Promise rejected without a value and emits an unhandled rejection warning.

question: What are middleware used for in an HTTP framework like Express?
options: To chain request and response processing, for example for auth, logging, or parsing data.
For native data storage in MongoDB, because middleware is Express's database layer and without it you cannot work with collections or models.
To transpile TypeScript to JavaScript at server runtime; Express middleware compiles `.ts` files on every request for the current endpoint.
To run the event loop in parallel, because each middleware runs on its own thread and Express therefore automatically scales across CPU cores.

question: What is a typical cause of memory leaks in Node.js?
options: Long-lived references that prevent the garbage collector from freeing no-longer-used data.
Every await automatically causes a memory leak, because while waiting the whole stack must be stored in memory and Node never frees it.
Streams never allocate memory, so memory leaks in Node usually come only from Buffers; if you don’t use Buffer, a leak cannot happen.
Memory leaks happen only in frontend; on the backend Node uses a "server GC" that always regularly clears memory regardless of references.

question: What is the difference between spawn and exec?
options: spawn is more suitable for streaming output, while exec buffers the entire output and returns it all at once.
exec can run only Node files and spawn only shell scripts; if you want to run a binary like ls, you must always use spawn and exec cannot do it.
spawn always blocks the event loop because it runs synchronously and waits for the process to finish, while exec is fully asynchronous and never blocks.
There is no difference between them; spawn is just an alias for exec and Node keeps two names for historical compatibility.

question: What are environment variables used for in a Node application?
options: To separate configuration from code, for example for secrets, URLs, or feature flags.
To store the request body during runtime, because env variables are the fastest storage and Node automatically sets them for each request and clears them after the request.
To replace a database: if you store data in environment variables, the application has them "persisted" and does not need any external storage.
To add new dependencies without a restart, because changing the NODE_PACKAGES env variable makes Node install packages at runtime via npm.

question: Why is it a good practice to validate input at the edge of the application?
options: It protects internal logic from invalid data and makes API behavior more predictable.
Because Node otherwise cannot work with JSON, and without validation JSON.parse would return random values that could crash the application.
Validation belongs only in the database, because only the database can guarantee correct types; if you validate in Node, it is pointless and can even hurt query performance.
Only to make the bundle smaller: validating input in Node reduces the size of the resulting JavaScript because unused code branches are eliminated.

question: What is the difference between a readable, writable, and transform stream?
options: A readable stream produces data, a writable stream consumes data, and a transform stream does both while transforming the data.
All three types are exactly the same; the difference is only in the class name, and in reality there is one universal stream that figures out by itself whether it should read or write.
A transform stream cannot be pipelined, because pipelines work only for pure readable/writable streams; transforms must always be wired manually using the data and end events.
A writable stream only reads files; the name "writable" means you can write configuration into it, but the data itself is always only read from disk.

question: What is pipeline() used for in the stream API?
options: To safely connect streams with better error handling and proper teardown.
pipeline() is used to define a middleware chain in Express; it is an internal API that Express uses for app.use() and route handlers.
To create a worker thread that processes each stream in parallel; pipeline automatically distributes the data stream across multiple threads based on the number of CPU cores.
To serialize a buffer into JSON, because pipeline() converts binary data to text and ensures the output is valid JSON regardless of content.

question: What can cause the event loop to be blocked?
options: A long-running synchronous CPU computation or synchronous I/O in a request handler.
Using Promise.all for multiple requests.
Normal use of async/await by itself.
Returning a JSON response to the client.

question: What is package.json used for?
options: It describes project metadata, dependencies, scripts, and other package settings.
It is an internal cache of the npm registry.
It contains only TypeScript types.
It replaces the lockfile.

question: Why is a lockfile (package-lock.json, bun.lock, pnpm-lock.yaml) important?
options: It pins specific dependency versions for reproducible installs.
It automatically speeds up application runtime.
It replaces tests in CI.
It is used only to store secrets.

question: What does the semver range ^1.2.3 mean?
options: It allows compatible minor and patch updates within the same major version.
It pins exactly the version 1.2.3 only.
It allows any later major version.
It means a prerelease package.

question: What are npm scripts used for?
options: To define reusable commands for build, tests, start, and other project tasks.
Only to install global package managers.
Only to define HTTP routes.
To disable the event loop in production.

question: What is the role of process.env?
options: It allows reading environment variables available to the process.
It contains only command-line arguments.
It is a global store for request data.
It is used exclusively for debug logs.

question: What does process.argv do?
options: It contains the command-line arguments the process was started with.
It returns a list of all Promises waiting in the event loop.
It stores environment variables.
It is an API for reading files.

question: What are __dirname or its ESM equivalent used for?
options: To derive the path to the current file or its directory for working with the file system.
To get the URL of the current HTTP request.
To get the package name from the npm registry.
To store a Buffer instance in the global scope.

question: What is a good reason to use structured logging?
options: Logs are easier to filter, aggregate, and analyze in observability tools.
Because string logs cannot be printed in Node at all.
Because structured logs automatically solve all errors.
Only to reduce memory footprint.

question: What is a healthcheck endpoint?
options: A simple endpoint signaling whether the service is basically available and ready to respond.
An endpoint for a full database export.
An endpoint that must return the entire application config.
A tool for installing npm packages.

question: What is the difference between a liveness and a readiness check?
options: Liveness indicates whether the process is alive; readiness indicates whether it is ready to serve traffic.
Readiness always replaces liveness.
Liveness is only for frontend and readiness only for backend.
There is no difference between them.

question: What is AbortController used for when working with fetch?
options: It allows canceling a request, for example on timeout or when an operation is canceled.
It is used to automatically retry the request.
It adds the response to a cache.
It replaces Promise.race.

question: What is the main risk of callback hell?
options: Worse readability, testability, and error handling with deeply nested callbacks.
Callbacks are always slower than Promises.
Callbacks cannot be used with the file system.
Callback hell applies only to CSS build pipelines.

question: When does it make sense to use Promise.all?
options: When you want to run multiple independent asynchronous operations in parallel and wait for all of them.
When operations must run strictly sequentially.
When you want to ignore errors from all Promises.
Only for a single request.

question: What is an appropriate approach to network call timeouts?
options: Have an explicit timeout or abort strategy and do not let requests hang forever.
Assume network requests always return quickly.
Handle timeouts only on the frontend.
Disable retries and logging.

question: What is HTTP keep-alive used for?
options: It allows reusing a TCP connection for multiple requests and reduces the overhead of establishing connections.
It ensures automatic encryption without TLS.
It removes the need for response headers.
It works only in browser fetch, not on the server.

question: What can be the consequence of a missing error handler on a stream?
options: The process may exit or an unhandled error can occur in the data processing flow.
The stream automatically switches to retry mode.
The stream changes to synchronous mode.
No consequence; streams ignore errors.

question: Why is graceful shutdown behavior important for a server?
options: It allows finishing in-flight requests, closing connections, and releasing resources when the process is terminating.
Only so that the package-lock can be overwritten.
Because Node cannot start an HTTP server without it.
It is needed only in tests.

question: What does an idempotent HTTP operation typically mean?
options: Repeating the same request has the same effect on the system state as executing it once.
The request is always asynchronous.
The request never returns an error.
The response must be empty.

question: What is rate limiting used for on an API server?
options: It protects the service against abuse, brute-force attacks, and overload.
It ensures server-side template rendering.
It replaces authentication.
It is used only for response compression.

question: Why is it dangerous to log sensitive values?
options: They can leak into log management systems or be accessible to people who should not have access to them.
Sensitive values are automatically encrypted in logs.
Logs are never stored in Node.
It is only a problem in the browser console.

question: What is the main benefit of schema validation for requests?
options: A clear input contract, earlier detection of errors, and safer data processing.
Node then doesn’t need tests.
Schema validation is only for GraphQL.
Validation reduces network latency by shortening the TCP handshake.

question: When is it appropriate to use database connection pooling?
options: When you don’t want to create a new connection for each request and you want to use resources more efficiently.
When you want to disable database authentication.
Only when using worker threads.
Pooling is suitable only for the file system.

question: What is the value of observability metrics in addition to logs?
options: Metrics make it easy to track trends, latency, error rate, and capacity over time.
Metrics fully replace the need for logging.
Metrics are intended only for frontend CSS.
Metrics cannot be used for alerting.

question: What is a typical use case for `Readable.from()`?
options: Creating a readable stream from an iterable source or collection.
Converting an HTTP server into a worker thread.
Generating TypeScript declarations.
Replacing a package manager.

question: What is an Angular component?
options: A fundamental building block of the UI that defines a template, logic, and behavioral metadata.
A special service intended exclusively for HTTP communication that automatically sends requests on every component render and stores responses in an internal cache.
A replacement for router configuration: an Angular component is actually a route definition, and without the router it cannot be used or displayed.
A CSS file with a decorator, where selectors and styles are defined using `@Component`, while a TypeScript class is not needed.

question: What is the `@Component` decorator used for?
options: To add metadata about the template, selectors, and other component properties.
To register a dependency in the injector tree: once you mark a class with `@Component`, Angular automatically registers it as a provider and it can then be injected into services.
To create an RxJS observable, because `@Component` turns the class into a data stream of changes and Angular then renders everything reactively by subscribing.
To mark a route guard so Angular Router knows the class blocks access to a certain URL and should run before navigation.

question: What is dependency injection in Angular?
options: A mechanism for providing service instances and injecting them into classes without manual instantiation.
A mandatory way to define CSS classes, where styles are "injected" into a component via a decorator; without DI you cannot style in Vue/React.
Runtime validation of all forms: Angular DI supposedly checks input types and automatically throws errors when the user enters an invalid value.
A mechanism for server-side rendering HTML where templates are "injected" into the resulting document on the server and nothing is rendered on the client.

question: What does a service typically represent in Angular?
options: Reusable business or integration logic separated from components.
Only an HTML template without TypeScript used for sharing UI fragments; a service is therefore basically a "partial" template.
A route defined in the router: each route must have its own service that holds the URL and Angular automatically attaches it into `router-outlet`.
An application module declaration, because a service is a synonym for NgModule and components, directives, and pipes are registered through services.

question: What is the benefit of standalone components in Angular?
options: They allow building an application without declaring everything in an NgModule, with more explicit imports.
They forbid dependency injection because standalone components are "isolated" and have no access to the injector tree; all dependencies must be written manually.
They work only in test mode; in production builds standalone components are automatically converted back into NgModules to make the bundle smaller.
They replace RxJS observables by introducing a new system of async values, and Angular then no longer supports `HttpClient` returning an observable.

question: What is `@Input()` used for?
options: To receive data from a parent component.
To emit events to the parent, because `@Input()` is two-way: a change in the child is automatically reflected back into the parent without events.
To register a service in the injector tree, where `@Input()` marks a constructor parameter as a provider and Angular then creates it as a singleton.
To define a route parameter: `@Input()` is used to read `:id` from the URL and the router automatically fills it during navigation.

question: What is `@Output()` used for?
options: To emit events from a child component up to the parent.
To receive data from a backend API, because `@Output()` is shorthand for `HttpClient.get()` and Angular automatically maps the response into events.
To create a form controller: each `@Output()` behaves like a `FormControl` and Angular Forms automatically registers it into the nearest `FormGroup`.
To declare a CSS animation, since output means "output to the DOM" and Angular adds a transition when the value changes.

question: What is `EventEmitter` in Angular?
options: An object commonly used with `@Output()` to emit events from a component.
A global application store that Angular provides automatically; `EventEmitter` holds state and anyone who subscribes always gets the latest value like in Redux.
A replacement for the HTTP client: instead of requests, you "emit" URLs and Angular downloads them in the background and sends the result back as an event.
A mechanism for lazy loading routes, where `EventEmitter` tells the router which modules to download; the router uses it internally for chunk splitting.

question: What is the difference between template-driven and reactive forms?
options: Template-driven forms are more declarative in the template, while reactive forms build the form model explicitly in TypeScript.
Reactive forms work only in SSR, because on the client Angular cannot maintain a form model and must rely on HTML attributes in the template-driven approach.
Template-driven forms cannot do validation; validators can be used only in reactive forms because they are tied to `FormControl` and the template has no API for it.
There is no practical difference; they are just different names for the same system and Angular always internally converts both approaches into a `FormGroup`.

question: When do reactive forms make more sense?
options: For more complex forms with more dynamic validation, composition, and better testability.
Only for checkboxes, because reactive forms are designed only for boolean values; text inputs must be template-driven.
When the form contains a single input, because reactive forms are the simplest option and template-driven is better only for large forms with many fields.
Only if the application does not use a router, because reactive forms take over navigation between pages and would conflict with the router.

question: What is a `FormControl`?
options: An object representing the state and validation of a single form field.
An Angular component for rendering an input (e.g., `<form-control>`), which creates an HTML `<input>` itself and automatically adds all validators based on type.
A route guard for URL validation: `FormControl` checks whether route parameters match a defined schema and if not, the router redirects elsewhere.
A CSS directive for styling an input that adds classes like `ng-valid/ng-invalid`, and without it Angular Forms cannot mark errors.

question: What is a `FormGroup`?
options: A collection of multiple form controls grouped into a single form model.
A list of services registered in a provider: `FormGroup` is an internal DI registry and Angular stores all injectable classes for forms in it.
A lazy-loading mechanism where `FormGroup` groups modules into one chunk and loads them only when a form is first used on a page.
A wrapper around an HTTP request that groups multiple requests into one transaction and sends them at once when the form is submitted.

question: What is a validator used for in a form?
options: To verify whether the input value meets the required rules.
To serialize the form into CSS: a validator "validates" selectors and then generates corresponding styles for error states directly into a `<style>` tag.
To register a component into a module, because without a validator a form component won’t be added to an NgModule and Angular won’t be able to use it in a template.
To recalculate the change detection tree, since a validator determines which components should be rechecked when an input changes and thereby optimizes performance.

question: What is Angular Router?
options: A mechanism that maps URLs to components and supports guards, lazy loading, and nested routes.
A replacement for dependency injection: in Angular the router creates all service and component instances, and without it DI doesn’t work even in a simple application.
A library for HTTP requests that routes requests to different backend servers based on URL; it is used instead of `HttpClient`.
A Node.js backend runtime that lets you run an Angular app on the server as Express middleware and handle API routes.

question: What is a route guard used for?
options: To decide whether a route can be activated, deactivated, or loaded based on defined logic.
To directly render child components, because a guard is a special type of component inserted into the DOM before the page and only then decides what to render.
To memoize change detection: a guard stores the result of the last render and restores it when returning to the route without re-rendering.
To serialize a form into the query string, since a guard "guards" the form state and automatically adds it to the URL during navigation.

question: What is lazy loading of routes in Angular?
options: Loading part of the application or a feature only when navigating to the corresponding route.
Slow switching between routes under low CPU; lazy loading means the router intentionally slows navigation to save battery on mobile.
Loading HTML via an iframe, where each route is loaded as a separate web page inside the app and Angular just switches the iframe URL.
A mechanism for disabling the bundler: with lazy loading Angular doesn’t use build steps and loads TypeScript files directly in the browser.

question: What is HttpClient used for?
options: For sending HTTP requests and working with their responses via observables.
For rendering templates, because HttpClient can download HTML from the internet and Angular then inserts it directly into the component instead of a local template.
For creating the dependency injector tree; HttpClient is the foundation of DI and all services are registered via httpClient.provide(...) similarly to other frameworks.
For working with DOM elements via ViewChild; the name HttpClient means "HTML client" and it is used for selecting elements and setting their attributes.

question: What is an HTTP interceptor?
options: A layer that can modify requests and responses or handle cross-cutting concerns like auth and logging.
A component for intercepting clicks (a click interceptor) that wraps the app and can block user actions based on rules defined in the router.
A guard that runs only on the 404 route to intercept non-existent URLs and redirect the user to the correct page before sending the request.
An RxJS operator for streaming DOM events, similar to fromEvent, that "intercepts" events and turns them into HTTP requests.

question: Why does Angular use RxJS?
options: To work with asynchronous data streams, events, and composition of operations over observables.
Only for styling components, because RxJS contains "reactive CSS" and Angular uses it to recompute styles when data changes.
Because Promises do not work in Angular: Angular removes them during the build, and without RxJS it would not be possible to write any asynchronous code.
Exclusively because of the DI container, since RxJS Observables are also provider registries and the Angular injector creates service instances from them.

question: What is an Observable?
options: A representation of a stream of values over time that can be transformed and subscribed to.
A one-time synchronous value that is computed immediately when created and never changes; it is basically an alias for a regular variable.
A decorator for registering components that "marks" a class as observable and Angular then automatically re-renders it without change detection.
An HTML element for forms (e.g., <observable>) that Angular adds to the DOM for debugging and displaying data streams on the page.

question: When do you need to unsubscribe from an observable?
options: When the subscription can outlive the component and there is a risk of a memory leak or unwanted side effects.
Never; Angular unsubscribes everything automatically in all cases, even if you manually subscribe in a component without any cleanup.
Only for HTTP requests that complete immediately, because those stay open and without unsubscribe the request would be sent forever.
Only for standalone components, because in apps with NgModule Angular handles unsubscribe, but standalone mode disables this functionality.

question: What does the async pipe do?
options: It subscribes to an observable or promise in the template and cleans up the subscription when the component is destroyed.
It forces synchronous reading of an observable: the async pipe stops the event loop and waits until the observable emits a value so the template always gets data "immediately".
It replaces the router outlet and switches entire pages based on the observable value; the async pipe is therefore a navigation mechanism for dynamic routing.
It is used only for template typing: it does nothing, it only adds type information for TypeScript and is completely removed at runtime.

question: What is the map operator used for in RxJS?
options: It transforms emitted values into new values.
It stops the observable stream by defining boundaries; after the first map the stream completes and emits nothing further.
