It shares a subscription between components: map creates one shared stream for multiple subscribers, preventing duplicate HTTP requests.
It creates a dependency injection map where keys are tokens and values are instances; Angular DI then uses this object when creating services.

question: What does switchMap typically do?
options: When a new input value arrives, it cancels the previous inner stream and switches to the new one.
It merges all inner streams in parallel without canceling, so it is suitable when you want to keep all requests and deliver their results in any order.
It converts an observable into a signal, eliminating the need to subscribe; switchMap is therefore a bridge between RxJS and Angular signals.
It attaches a CSS module to a component: based on the stream value it switches active styles and remaps classes in the template.

question: When is mergeMap more appropriate than switchMap?
options: When you want to keep multiple nested asynchronous operations running in parallel.
When you always want to cancel the previous request, because mergeMap automatically disconnects old streams and keeps only the latest one (unlike switchMap).
Only for synchronous streams; if you map to an HTTP request (async) inside, mergeMap must not be used and you must always use switchMap.
Only in route guards, because mergeMap works only with Angular Router; in components it is invalid and TypeScript will not allow it.

question: What does change detection mean in Angular?
options: A mechanism by which Angular detects data changes and reflects them in the template.
Generating a new bundle after a file changes, i.e., Angular's internal "watch" mechanism (via Zone.js) that re-bundles JavaScript and reloads it into the page on every change in the app.
Runtime type checking in TypeScript.
A mechanism for diffing database migrations.

question: What does the OnPush strategy provide?
options: It limits default change detection to more explicit scenarios and can reduce unnecessary recomputations.
It forces a re-render on every tick because the component switches into an "aggressive" mode that re-checks the whole subtree on every microtask (Promise, timer) just in case.
It works only in template-driven forms.
It replaces the need for @Input().

question: What is a common reason an OnPush component does not update?
options: Mutating an object in place instead of passing a new reference, or not triggering change detection appropriately.
Angular with OnPush cannot render arrays.
OnPush works only on the server.
OnPush automatically ignores all events (click, input, output emit), so the UI does not react until you explicitly call detectChanges() in every handler.

question: What is ChangeDetectorRef used for?
options: For finer control over change detection, for example to manually mark a component for checking.
To inject the route into a child component, because ChangeDetectorRef internally holds a reference to the current ActivatedRoute and allows switching routes without using the Router service.
For working with DOM events in CSS.
To create an observable from a signal.

question: What is an Angular signal?
options: A reactive primitive representing a readable and writable value with dependency tracking.
A type of route guard.
A replacement for dependency injection.
A mechanism for SSR caching where a "signal" acts as a server cache key and Angular uses it to share rendered HTML between users.

question: What is the relationship between signals and change detection?
options: Signals can help express dependencies more precisely and better control UI updates.

Signals disable template rendering: if you use a signal, Angular no longer computes template changes via change detection and the template stops re-rendering until you manually "wake it up" by calling render().
Signals work only outside components.
Signals are just an alias for Promises.

question: What is a pipe in Angular?
options: A mechanism for transforming a value in a template, for example a date or number formatting.

A replacement for an RxJS operator: an Angular pipe is basically the same as rxjs.pipe(), so you can write operators like map/switchMap directly in the template and Angular will evaluate them over the stream.
An API for an HTTP request pipeline.
A component for lazy loading.

question: When does a custom pipe make sense?
options: When you want to repeatedly and readably convert data into a presentation format in templates.
When you need to manage dependency injection, because a custom pipe is the recommended way to register and configure providers (it is "lighter" than a service).
When you want to mutate a form model.
Only for route params.

question: What is ngFor used for?
options: To iterate over a collection and render repeated elements in a template.
For conditionally rendering a single element.
To register a new service.
To define scoped CSS styles: ngFor supposedly creates a unique scope for each iteration and automatically rewrites selectors so styles do not leak between list items.

question: What is ngIf used for?
options: To conditionally display or remove part of the template based on an expression.
To iterate over an observable: *ngIf="items$" supposedly works like a "for-each" over emissions and creates a new template instance on each emission.
To inject route guards.
To create an output event.

question: Why is it good to use trackBy for large lists?
options: It allows Angular to reuse DOM elements more efficiently and reduce unnecessary changes when updating the list.
It automatically adds unique CSS classes to items (e.g., .item-42) based on the trackBy key, making items easy to style without extra attributes.
It replaces the need for ngFor.
It works only with signals.

question: What is ViewChild?
options: A mechanism for getting a reference to a child component, directive, or DOM element from the template.
A way to pass data from child to parent: @ViewChild supposedly automatically "pulls" selected values out of the child and synchronizes them into the parent without needing @Output().
A replacement for a route resolver.
An RxJS operator for working with child observables.

question: What is the ngOnInit lifecycle hook used for?
options: Initialization logic after the component is created and inputs are set.
Cleaning up subscriptions on destroy, because ngOnInit is called when the component is detached from the DOM and it is the recommended place to unsubscribe listeners.
Catching route errors.
Creating an Angular module.

question: When is ngOnDestroy used?
options: For cleaning up subscriptions, timers, or other resources before a component or service is destroyed.
For setting default props.
Only during server-side rendering, because in the browser components never "die" and Angular only hides them, so ngOnDestroy is not called on the client.
To register a lazy route.

question: What is the main role of a resolver in the router?
options: Prepare data before activating the route so the component has it available upon entry.
Verify the page’s CSS validity.
Replace all HTTP interceptors: supposedly a resolver wraps all requests for a given route, adds headers and logging, so interceptors are no longer needed.
Convert an observable to a promise without waiting.

question: What is `router-outlet` used for?
options: Marks the place in the template where the component of the current route is rendered.
Creates a new dependency injector.
Converts a URL into a query string.
Stores navigation history in localStorage so that after a page reload the navigation stack is restored and the user returns to the same screen without any additional configuration.

question: What is the difference between the `CanActivate` and `CanDeactivate` guards?
options: `CanActivate` decides whether a route can be entered; `CanDeactivate` decides whether it can be left.
`CanActivate` is for SSR and `CanDeactivate` for CSR: supposedly only `CanActivate` is evaluated on the server, while only `CanDeactivate` is used on the client to save computations.
Both guards only handle lazy loading of modules.
There is no difference between them.

question: Why is it often beneficial to separate smart and presentational components?
options: It improves readability, testability, and separation of concerns between data and presentation.
Otherwise Angular will not run change detection.
Presentational components must not have a template.
Smart components cannot be routed.

question: What is the main benefit of Angular CLI?
options: It standardizes building, scaffolding, testing, and project configuration.
It replaces all npm packages with a single command.
It is used exclusively for SSR rendering.
It automatically creates a backend API.

question: What does `providedIn: 'root'` do for a service?
options: Registers the service in the root injector tree and typically makes it a singleton for the application.
Restricts the service to a single component.
It is used to configure routing.
Ensures that the service is never initialized.

question: What is the main risk of tightly coupling components via services with mutable state?
options: It can create hidden coupling, less predictable behavior, and more difficult debugging.
Angular will then disable dependency injection.
Services automatically become immutable.
Mutable state can only be held in forms.

question: What are testing utilities like TestBed used for?
options: To create an Angular testing environment for components, services, and DI configuration.
For production bootstrapping of the application.
For directly deploying the build to a server.
To convert observables to signals.

question: Why is it important not to overload a template with complex logic?
options: It hurts readability, testability, and performance, because a template should primarily serve declarative presentation.
Because Angular templates do not support expressions.
Because everything must always be in CSS.
Only so that the router works.

question: What is the main goal of server-side rendering in an Angular application?
options: Improve first paint, SEO, and content availability before full client hydration.
Replace the need for browser JavaScript in all scenarios.
Disable routing on the client.
Fix a memory leak in services.

question: What is the typical difference between `Subject` and `BehaviorSubject`?
options: `BehaviorSubject` holds and immediately provides the latest value to new subscribers; `Subject` does not.
`Subject` is immutable and `BehaviorSubject` is a mutable object.
`BehaviorSubject` only works in the router.
`Subject` cannot be used in a service.

question: What is the main difference between `any` and `unknown`?
options: `unknown` requires type narrowing or checks before use, while `any` disables type checking.
`any` can only be used for strings and `unknown` only for objects, because TypeScript separates “text” and “data” values for compilation optimization.
`unknown` is the same as `never`, because both types mean “you can’t work with it” and TypeScript internally treats them as equivalent.
`any` is safer than a precisely defined type, because it lets TypeScript avoid errors and thus reduces the risk of runtime exceptions.

question: What does the `never` type typically represent?
options: A state that should never occur, or a function that never returns normally.
Any value without restrictions, i.e. the same as `any` but with stricter editor checks; `never` is used when you want to allow absolutely everything.
Any value except `null`, i.e. a “non-null” type that guarantees a variable is always defined and can be used safely without checks.
An alias for `void` mainly used with async functions; `never` means the function returns nothing but completes successfully.

question: What is a union type?
options: A type that can be one of several specified variants.
A type created by combining the properties of multiple objects together, i.e. the “sum” of all properties; a union is used when you want to merge two objects into a single larger one.
A type meant exclusively for arrays, because union means “unification” of elements in an array into a common type, and it is not used outside collections.
A mechanism for runtime JSON validation where the union type automatically checks the object structure during parsing and throws an exception on error.

question: What is an intersection type?
options: A type that combines the requirements of multiple types at the same time.
A type for selecting exactly one of several variants; an intersection works like XOR and TypeScript then enforces that an object matches exactly one branch.
A type meant only for string literals; an intersection is used to join strings (e.g. `'a' & 'b'` becomes `'ab'`).
A type for asynchronous functions: an intersection indicates that a function returns multiple values at once and TypeScript turns that into a `Promise`.

question: What is type narrowing?
options: Narrowing a broader type down to a more specific variant using checks in code.
Automatically adding new properties to an object based on which code branches executed; TypeScript then “expands” the object’s type at runtime.
Converting TypeScript to JavaScript during the build; narrowing is a compiler optimization step that shortens the output by removing types.
Shortening type names for performance so the editor shows IntelliSense faster; narrowing compresses types into shorter aliases.

question: What is a type guard used for?
options: To verify or declare that a value matches a more specific type.
To protect imports from circular dependencies, because a type guard prevents a module from loading if it would create a cycle and thus avoids runtime errors.
To create a readonly object: a type guard locks a value so it can no longer change, and TypeScript then enforces immutability at runtime as well.
To serialize a type to JSON, where a type guard exports the type structure as a schema and the app can send it to the backend.

question: What is the practical difference between `interface` and `type`?
options: Both can describe objects, but `type` also represents unions/aliases better, and `interface` supports declaration merging.
`interface` can only be used for classes and `type` only for functions; if you want to type an object literal, you must use `type` because an interface cannot do that.
`type` is evaluated at runtime and exists in JS as a real object, while `interface` exists only at build time and cannot be used in runtime code.
There is no difference in usage or capabilities; they are just two syntaxes for the same thing and TypeScript always internally converts them to the same AST node.

question: What does `extends` do in a generic constraint?
options: Constrains the generic parameter to types that satisfy the given condition.
Enforces inheritance only for class components; if you pass an interface or a primitive as the generic, `extends` is ignored and the compiler skips it.
Converts a union into an intersection.
Ensures runtime input validation: when the program runs, TypeScript checks that `T` really extends the constraint, and if not, it throws an exception.

question: What are generics used for?
options: To write reusable, type-safe functions, classes, and utilities that work with different types.
To compile TypeScript into JavaScript.
To import types from an external library without `import` statements, because generics can automatically “pull” them from npm packages by type name.
To disable inference in the editor: if you use generics, TypeScript stops inferring types and you must always specify all of them explicitly.

question: What does `keyof T` mean?
options: A union of the key names of type `T`.
Returns the type of values in object `T`, i.e. equivalent to `T[string]`; it is mainly used to get the union of all value types.
Gets only the required properties of type `T` and discards all optional keys, because `keyof` works only with required properties.
Creates a readonly copy of type `T`.

question: What is `typeof` used for in a type position?
options: It allows deriving a type from an existing value or symbol.
It converts a runtime value into a string even in a type position; for example `typeof 123` becomes the type `'number'` and can then be used as a string literal type.
It replaces `instanceof` in JavaScript by performing a runtime prototype check.
It works only for primitive types.

question: What is an indexed access type like `T[K]`?
options: The type of the value at key `K` in type `T`.
An operation for array access only at runtime, similar to `arr[k]`, which TypeScript adds to the resulting JavaScript as safe access with index checking.
An alias for `keyof T`, because the expression `T[K]` always returns key names and is used only as a more convenient way to get the union of keys.
It always returns `string`, because indexed access in TypeScript is internally represented as a string map and numbers/booleans are first converted to strings.

question: What is a mapped type?
options: A type created by iterating over the keys of another type and transforming their properties.
A type intended only for map collections (`Map`/`WeakMap`); without using these structures, mapped types do not work and the compiler ignores them.
A runtime API for converting objects, similar to `Object.map()`, which TypeScript adds to the global scope so that object properties can be mapped at runtime.
A mechanism for deduplicating union types that removes duplicate variants at compile time and sorts the union alphabetically to make the type “cleaner”.

question: What is a conditional type?
options: A type defined with a condition in the form `T extends U ? X : Y`.
A type that can only be used inside an `if` block because it is tied to control flow analysis and outside conditions TypeScript does not evaluate conditional types.
An alias for an optional property, because conditional types are just another notation for `?` on properties and are used to make a property optional depending on compiler settings.
A type available only in `.d.ts` files; in normal `.ts` files conditional types cannot be used because they would slow down the build.

question: What is the utility type `Partial<T>` used for?
options: Makes all properties of type `T` optional.
Makes all properties of type `T` readonly and also “locks” them at runtime, so when you try to change them TypeScript adds `Object.freeze()`.
Selects only half of the properties of type `T` (e.g., the first 50% by declaration order) and discards the rest to simplify the type.
Removes all methods from the type and leaves only data properties.

question: What is `Required<T>` used for?
options: Makes all properties of type `T` required.
Selects only required properties and deletes the rest from the runtime object, so after compilation optional properties truly disappear and the object becomes smaller.
Adds default values to an object: `Required<T>` generates an initializer function that fills in missing values based on the type and prevents `undefined`.
Enforces that the type must contain at least one method; if the type has no methods, `Required<T>` marks it as invalid and compilation fails with an error.

question: What is `Pick<T, K>` used for?
options: Creates a new type containing only the selected keys `K` from type `T`.
Removes keys `K` from type `T` (it is the opposite of `Pick`), so the resulting type contains everything except the listed properties.
Selects the first value from a union type, because “pick” means to choose one variant; `Pick<A | B, ...>` always reduces the union to a single branch.
Makes all properties optional because it selects only those that are optional; required properties cannot be used in `Pick`.

question: What is `Omit<T, K>` used for?
options: Creates a new type without keys `K` from type `T`.
Makes keys `K` readonly, because `Omit` means “to restrict” changes; the properties remain but cannot be overwritten.
Selects only keys `K`, meaning it behaves the same as `Pick`; in TypeScript `Pick` and `Omit` are just historical aliases for the same utility type.
Adds a new key `K` to type `T`, because omitting in practice means “adding the omitted keys” back into the object.

question: What is `Record<K, V>` used for?
options: Creates an object type with keys `K` and values of type `V`.
Converts an array into a tuple, because a record is an entry with a fixed length; `Record` is used primarily for typing tuple structures.
Enables serializing a type to JSON; `Record` adds metadata to the type that can then be read at runtime and automatically create a JSON schema.
Works only for string values, because `Record` is internally defined as `Record<string, string>` and TypeScript does not allow other value types.

question: What does `ReturnType<T>` do?
options: Gets the return type of function `T`.
Gets the type of the first argument of function `T` and ignores the rest; used when you want to enforce the same input type for another function.
Makes a function readonly so it cannot be called more than once; `ReturnType` marks the function as pure and TypeScript then forbids its side effects.
Enforces that a function returns a Promise; `ReturnType` automatically wraps the return type in `Promise<...>` and makes async the standard.

question: What is the `infer` keyword used for?
options: To capture and infer part of a type within conditional types.
To declare interface implementation, similar to `implements`; `infer` tells the compiler that a class satisfies a given type and code should be generated accordingly.
To manually disable inference in an entire file so TypeScript does not recompute types and the build is faster; types then have to be written explicitly everywhere.
To translate TS types into runtime validators, where `infer` generates functions that verify data structure at runtime against defined types.

question: What does `foo?: string` mean?
options: The property may be missing or be of type `string` according to TypeScript rules and compiler settings.
The property must always be `null` because `?` means “nullable” and TypeScript enforces that the value is either `null` or compilation stops with an error.
The property is readonly; the question mark is shorthand for “immutable”, so `foo?: string` means the property can never be changed after object initialization.
The property must be a function returning a string, because `?` marks an optional call signature and TypeScript uses it for typing methods.

question: When is the `!` non-null assertion risky?
options: When it silences type checking without certainty that the value truly is not `null` or `undefined`.
When used inside an interface, because `!` in a type position changes the type to `never` and can make the interface impossible to implement.
When working with `string`, because non-null assertion on strings changes the value to an empty string and this can cause silent UI bugs.
It is never risky, because `!` only helps the compiler and TypeScript also automatically adds a runtime check that throws a safe error on `null/undefined`.

question: What is a type assertion like `value as Foo`?
options: An instruction to the compiler on how to treat a value type-wise; it does not perform runtime validation by itself.
A safe runtime conversion of a value to another type.
Required syntax for every API response.
A way to create a generic type.

question: What does `readonly` on a property mean?
options: In TypeScript, it prevents reassignment after initialization at the type level.
It completely locks the property at runtime via `Object.freeze`.
It allows the property to be deleted.
It automatically makes the property optional.

question: What is a discriminated union?
options: A union of types sharing a discriminant key that can be used to safely narrow to a specific variant.
An intersection of two interfaces with the same name.
A type intended only for enums.
A mechanism for serializing a union into a string.

question: How do you verify exhaustiveness over a discriminated union?
options: For example, using a `switch` and a helper with the `never` type in the default branch.
Using `JSON.stringify`.
Only using runtime `instanceof`.
Exhaustiveness cannot be handled in TypeScript.

question: What are literal types used for?
options: They let you work with specific values as types, for example `'open' | 'closed'`.
They are only for typing numeric arrays.
They are runtime constants generated by the compiler.
They replace the need for enums in all cases.

question: What does `as const` do?
options: Narrows a value to the most specific literal types possible and marks nested properties as readonly.
Compiles an object into an interface.
Forces a deep clone of an object.
Turns an array into a mutable list.

question: What is a tuple?
options: An array with a fixed, defined number of elements and element types at specific positions.
An object with dynamic string keys.
An alias for a union of string literals.
A special Promise type.

question: When do function overloads make sense?
options: When a single function supports multiple combinations of parameters and return types with different contracts.
When we want to enforce asynchronous behavior of a function.
Only when exporting a default class.
An overload is required for every generic function.

question: What does structural typing mean in TypeScript?
options: Type compatibility is determined by shape and members, not by a type’s nominal identity.
Types are compatible only when they inherit from the same class.
Every type must have a unique runtime identifier.
TypeScript compares types only by name.

question: What is the `satisfies` operator used for?
options: Checks that a value matches a given type without unnecessarily widening or overriding the inferred type of the value.
Converts an object to `unknown`.
Replaces `implements` for classes.
Works only for enums.

question: What is declaration merging?
options: The ability for multiple declarations with the same name in some cases to be merged into a single type.
Automatic merging of all imports in a file.
Runtime merging of two objects into one.
A feature available only in JavaScript.

question: What does `strictNullChecks` do?
options: Forces explicit handling of `null` and `undefined` instead of silently ignoring them.
Prohibits using optional properties because optional values are internally represented as `T | undefined`.
Adds a runtime check to every `if` to prevent `null`/`undefined` from entering code branches.
Ensures that no variable is ever `undefined` because the compiler automatically supplies default values and narrows types so that `undefined` cannot “leak” into the code.

question: What does `noImplicitAny` mean?
options: The compiler reports errors where a type ends up as an implicit `any`.
All explicit `any` types are automatically forbidden at runtime and replaced with the `never` type during compilation to prevent calling such values.
TypeScript stops inferring function types and requires every function to have explicit annotations for both parameters and return values.
It changes all missing types to `unknown` to prevent `any` leaks and force subsequent narrowing using type guards.

question: What does `strict` mode in `tsconfig` address?
options: Enables a set of stricter type checks for safer and more precise type handling.
Compiles TypeScript into “strict” JavaScript mode, i.e., it automatically adds `'use strict'` at the top of every file regardless of `target` and bundler settings.
Disables the ability to use generics.
It is intended only for test builds: in production it is commonly turned off to speed up compilation and reduce the size of the resulting JavaScript.

question: What does it mean that `implements` on a class is only a compile-time contract?
options: TypeScript checks conformance to the type contract, but does not generate any special runtime behavior because of it.
`implements` automatically creates instances of all methods defined in the interface (if missing) so the class always fulfills the contract even without manual implementation.
`implements` works only with an abstract class, because with an interface it is not possible to verify member existence at runtime.
`implements` adds metadata to the object.

question: What is a default generic parameter used for?
options: It allows a generic to use a default type if the caller does not provide another type.
It disables generic type inference, because when a default exists TypeScript prefers the default type and does not try to infer the type from arguments.
It is used only for typing class decorators.
It converts a generic type into a union.

question: What is the advantage of inferring a generic type from a function argument?
options: The caller often doesn’t need to write the type parameter manually, yet still gets an exact type result.
It makes the function asynchronous, because inference happens “later” and TypeScript evaluates it internally only after runtime values are resolved.
Inference always returns `unknown`.
It works only for arrays of strings.

question: What are template literal types used for?
options: Creating new string types by composing other literal types.
Rendering an HTML template on the server, because TypeScript can generate the corresponding runtime templates from these types.
Writing multi-line strings at runtime: the type automatically enforces correct indentation and escaping of characters like `\n` and `\t`.
Only for typing CSS modules: TypeScript uses them exclusively for naming classes and they are not suitable for general string-type composition.

question: What is a distributive conditional type?
options: A conditional type that, when used over a union type, is applied to each member separately.
A conditional type that is applied only to the first member of a union to improve compiler performance for large union types.
A type that can only be used in a distributed system (e.g., microservices), because it requires special compilation settings for multiple packages.
A mechanism for splitting an object into two types (e.g., `Pick` and `Omit`) using a condition that separates required and optional keys.

question: When does it make sense to use a branded type?
options: When we want to distinguish two values with the same runtime representation but different meaning, for example `UserId` and `OrderId`.
When we want to make an object a readonly type.
Only when defining enums.
Branded types are required for every API DTO.

question: What is a common problem with overly aggressive use of `as`?
options: It can bypass type safety and hide errors that then show up only at runtime.
The code then cannot be compiled to JavaScript.
TypeScript then disables all utility types.
`as` automatically changes the runtime shape of the object.

question: How do `null` and `undefined` differ in TypeScript?
options: They are two distinct values and, with `strictNullChecks` enabled, also distinct type cases.
They are always interchangeable and TypeScript does not distinguish them.
`undefined` can only be used in the browser.
`null` is forbidden in TypeScript.

question: What is optional chaining `?.` used for?
options: It allows you to safely access the next member only if the previous value is neither `null` nor `undefined`.
It automatically converts `null` to an empty string.
It replaces the need for type narrowing throughout the entire function.
It works only for Promises.

question: What does nullish coalescing `??` do?
options: Returns the right-hand side only when the left-hand side is `null` or `undefined`, not for every falsy value.
Returns the right-hand side for `0` and an empty string just like `||`.
It is used to create union types.
It is only a type-level construct with no runtime behavior.

question: What is the benefit of `readonly` arrays and utilities like `ReadonlyArray<T>`?
options: They prevent mutating operations on a collection at the type level in places where we want to preserve immutability.
They automatically deep-clone data on every read.
They can be used only with tuples.
They mean the array cannot be iterated.

question: What is the purpose of the utility type `Extract<T, U>`?
options: Takes from type `T` only those parts that are compatible with `U`.
Removes from `T` all parts compatible with `U`.
Creates a readonly copy of `T`.
Returns the first property of the object type `T`.

question: What does `Exclude<T, U>` do?
options: Removes from type `T` those parts that are compatible with `U`.
Selects from `T` only parts compatible with `U`.
Changes `T` to an intersection with `U`.
Creates a runtime validator from `T`.

question: What does the CSS cascade determine?
options: How it decides which rule applies when multiple styles target the same element.
How the browser orders HTML elements in the DOM.
How JavaScript modules are loaded: the CSS cascade is the ES module algorithm that determines in what order `import` runs and how dependencies are resolved.
How CSS variables are serialized into the resulting HTML so they can be sent from server to client and restored after a page refresh.

question: What is CSS specificity?
options: A mechanism that determines the strength of a selector when multiple rules conflict.
The number of properties written in one rule: the more declarations you have in the `{ ... }` block, the higher specificity the rule gets.
The number of media queries in a file; if CSS has many breakpoints, the browser increases specificity so styles “stick” better when the width changes.
The type of the browser’s rendering engine (e.g., Blink/WebKit/Gecko), because each engine has different “specificity” and may prefer different selectors.

question: Which selector generally has higher specificity?
options: `#header .item`
`.item` (a class has higher specificity than an ID in CSS because it is used more often and is therefore considered a “stronger” selector).
`div`
`*` (the universal selector has the highest specificity because it matches all elements and therefore must override other rules).

question: What does `!important` do?
options: Artificially increases a declaration’s priority in the cascade, so it should be used sparingly.
Makes a property animatable, so transitions (`transition`) start working even for properties that normally cannot be animated.
Speeds up style rendering in the browser.
Works only in inline styles; if you use `!important` in an external CSS file, the browser ignores it.

question: What is the box model?
options: A model that includes the content, padding, border, and margin around an element.
A mechanism for flex layout that divides an element into “boxes” and automatically stretches items in a row without needing `display: flex`.
An API for working with CSS variables.
A type of CSS selector for containers (e.g., `.box-model > .item`) used to select elements by their dimensions.

question: What does `box-sizing: border-box` mean?
options: The element’s width and height include padding and border.
The border is drawn outside the layout, so it takes up no space and never affects the element’s dimensions or its surroundings.
Margin is included in the content, so `width` means “content + margin” and the browser reduces the inner space accordingly.
The element ignores padding: the `border-box` value disables padding completely and the browser no longer considers it when calculating the box or drawing it.

question: What is the difference between margin and padding?
options: Margin creates space outside the element; padding creates space inside around the content.
Margin is inside and padding is outside the element; padding increases the “outer spacing” from neighboring elements, while margin increases space for content.
They are synonyms and the browser interprets them the same; the difference is only historical and depends on whether you write styles for block or inline elements.
Padding can be used only for text because it defines inner indentation of lines; for boxes and images only margin is used.

question: What is `display: flex` used for?
options: For one-dimensional layout of items in a row or column with flexible positioning.
To create a two-dimensional table similar to grid.
For absolute positioning of children: once you set `display: flex`, all children switch to `position: absolute` and can be moved via `top/left`.
Only for centering text, because flexbox was designed solely as a replacement for `text-align: center` and is not used for multi-element layout.

question: What does `justify-content` do in Flexbox?
options: Aligns items along the container’s main axis.
Always aligns items vertically regardless of `flex-direction`, so it is a universal “vertical align” property for Flexbox.
Sets their width so that all items stretch to the same size; `justify-content` therefore works like an automatic `width`.
Affects only grid layout; in Flexbox `justify-content` is ignored and only `align-content` is used instead.

question: What does `align-items` do in Flexbox?
options: Aligns items along the container’s cross axis.
Controls the order of flex items; `align-items` reorders children according to their `order` and automatically sorts them from smallest to largest.
Sets the gap between rows, i.e. it is equivalent to `row-gap`; if you have `flex-wrap`, `align-items` determines the distance between rows.
Works only with `position: absolute`, because alignment along the cross axis is only possible for absolutely positioned elements.

question: What is `gap` used for?
options: Sets spacing between items in flex or grid without needing margin hacks.
Adds space only around the last element in the container so it doesn’t touch the edge when scrolling; spacing between other elements does not change.
Replaces `padding` inside an element, so `gap` is universal inner spacing and works even with a single child element.
Works only for text nodes and adds spaces between words; for layout of block elements only `margin` is used.

question: What is the main difference between Flexbox and Grid?
options: Flexbox is primarily one-dimensional layout, Grid is two-dimensional.
Grid works only on the server and Flexbox only in the browser; with SSR you must use Grid because Flexbox requires client-side JavaScript.
Flexbox cannot center because it has no alignment properties; centering is only possible in CSS Grid.
Grid does not support responsive design because it has a fixed number of rows and columns; for responsive layout only Flexbox is always used.

question: What is `display: grid` used for?
options: To define a two-dimensional grid with rows and columns.
`display: grid` disables an element’s layout: it removes the box model and the browser stops measuring and rendering its children.
To replace media queries, because Grid automatically detects device width and recalculates columns without breakpoints.
Only for positioning via pixel coordinates; Grid is basically a visual wrapper around `position: absolute`.

question: What does `grid-template-columns` do?
options: Defines the column structure of a grid container.
Sets the number of rows in Flexbox, because `grid-template-columns` determines how many times a flex row should wrap to the next line.
Automatically adds borders between columns; the browser draws lines between tracks and there’s no need to use `border` on individual items.
Determines the order of HTML elements in the DOM: based on values in `grid-template-columns`, elements are reordered in the document for screen readers too.

question: What does the `fr` unit mean in CSS Grid?
options: A fraction of the available free space in the grid container.
A fixed number of pixels, just written as `fr` (e.g. `1fr` = `100px`) to make code more readable for grid layouts.
A percentage of viewport height (vh), i.e. relative column height; `fr` is specifically for vertical dimensions because Grid works in 2D.
A font unit (font ratio) that changes text size depending on the number of columns in the grid.

question: When should you use `minmax()` in Grid?
options: When you want to define a track’s minimum and maximum size in a flexible way.
When you want to disable responsive behavior, because `minmax()` locks track sizes and prevents Grid from recalculating sizes when the viewport changes.
When you need to absolutely position a child element; `minmax()` defines the minimum and maximum `top/left` coordinates for an item in the grid.
Only for animations.

question: What does `position: relative` do?
options: Allows shifting an element relative to its normal position and creates a positioning context for absolutely positioned descendants.
Always removes the element from the normal document flow, freeing up its space and moving other elements as if it didn’t exist.
Centers the element in the viewport, because “relative” means relative to the browser window; it works as a centering shorthand.
Fixes the element during scrolling so it moves with the top edge of the page and stays visible.

question: What does `position: absolute` do?
options: Removes an element from the normal flow and positions it relative to the nearest suitable positioning context.
Keeps the element always at the top of the viewport, because `absolute` means absolute to the window, so the element behaves like `position: fixed`.
Works the same as `sticky`.
Can be used only in Grid, because absolute positioning needs grid cells; outside `display: grid` `position: absolute` is ignored.

question: How does `position: sticky` work?
options: The element behaves as relative until it reaches a defined offset, then it sticks within its scroll container.
It is always fixed relative to the viewport regardless of parents; sticky is just another word for `fixed` and browsers map it to the same implementation.
It is an alias for `absolute` that only adds a default `top: 0`; therefore sticky is used mainly for tooltips and dropdowns.
It works only on the `<header>` element, because sticky positioning is a special feature intended for nav bars and other tags do not support it.

question: What is a stacking context?
options: A context that determines how elements are stacked on the Z axis relative to each other.
A list of all `z-index` values in the document that can be printed in DevTools; the browser maintains it as a table and uses it to decide what is on top.
A mechanism for CSS variable inheritance where variables are stacked “on top of each other” and the highest definition overwrites values from lower layers.
A grid layer for auto-placement that determines the stacking order of grid items into cells when rows and columns are not explicitly defined.

question: Why does `z-index` sometimes “not work”?
options: Because the element is not in the expected stacking context or does not have the appropriate positioning conditions set.
Because `z-index` works only on flex items; if an element is not a direct child of a flex container, the browser ignores the value.
Because the browser ignores values greater than 10 to prevent extreme layering; for higher layers you must use `z-index: max`.
Because `z-index` can be used only with `display: grid`; outside a grid container layering is disabled and order is determined only by the DOM.

question: What are media queries used for?
options: To apply styles based on device or viewport characteristics, such as screen width.
To asynchronously load styles from an API: a media query calls an endpoint for the given width and the browser downloads the corresponding CSS only after evaluating the condition.
To transform the DOM tree so that based on screen width HTML elements are rearranged in the document and the browser physically moves them to a different place in the DOM.
Only to set font-size, because media queries are designed only for typography and are not used for layout (flex/grid).

question: What is the mobile-first approach?
options: Designing the base styles for smaller devices and progressively enhancing them for larger breakpoints.
Styling only for mobile devices without a desktop version, because mobile-first means desktop is no longer supported and the display on large screens is intentionally the same as on a phone.
Always starting with a desktop layout and then hiding everything, because mobile-first is about “trimming” everything unnecessary from desktop for mobile performance.
Using only `max-width` media queries, because mobile-first is defined by writing styles from large screens down to smaller ones.

question: When is it useful to use `rem` instead of `px`?
options: When you want scaling based on the root font-size and better accessibility and responsiveness.
When you need a fixed value independent of user settings, because `rem` is an absolute unit locked to 16px and never changes.
Only for border-radius, because `rem` may be used in CSS only for rounded corners and the browser ignores it for margin/padding.
`rem` cannot be used for spacing; it works only for `font-size` and other properties do not support it.

question: What is the difference between `em` and `rem`?
options: `em` is based on the font-size of the current context; `rem` is based on the root element.
`em` is always a fixed pixel value and `rem` is a percentage, so `em` is suitable for precise values and `rem` for relative layout.
`rem` works only for text (`font-size`/`line-height`) and cannot be used for margin, padding, or width, whereas `em` supports these properties normally.
There is no difference; `em` and `rem` are two names for the same unit and differ only in how browsers display them in DevTools.

question: What does `overflow: hidden` do?
options: Hides content that overflows the element’s content box.
Disables scrolling for the entire page.
Always moves content into a new stacking context.
Fixes the element’s height to the viewport.

question: What is `object-fit: cover` used for?
options: Allows an image to fill its box while preserving aspect ratio, even at the cost of cropping.
Stretches an image without preserving aspect ratio.
Works only for background images; for a regular `` it is ignored because an image is already a “replaced element” and is controlled only by `background-size`.
Adds border-box sizing to the image.

question: What is a CSS custom property?
options: A user-defined CSS variable written for example as `--color-primary`.
A selector type for the root element.
A JavaScript variable available in CSS without explicit passing, which the browser automatically “maps” from global scope into styles.
Just an alias for a SASS variable.

question: What is the benefit of CSS variables compared to hardcoded values?
options: They improve reusability, theming, and centralized management of values like colors or spacing.
They automatically optimize the bundle to half the size.
They work only in preprocessors, because plain CSS cannot evaluate variables and SASS/LESS must always “recompute” them at build time.
They cannot be combined with media queries.

question: What does inheritance mean in CSS?
options: Some properties are inherited from parent to child elements, others are not.
All properties are always inherited; some just get overridden by higher specificity, so it looks like they are not inherited.
Inheritance works only for flex containers.
Inheritance applies only to CSS modules.

question: What is a common cause of unintended layout shifts?
options: Late-loaded resources without reserved space, such as images without dimensions.
Using `display: block`.
Setting `box-sizing: border-box`.
Using CSS variables, because they are evaluated only after CSSOM loads and therefore recompute layout later, which shifts elements.

question: What is the `:focus-visible` pseudo-class used for?
options: Styling the focus state primarily in cases where focus is relevant for keyboard navigation.
Hiding focus for all users, because `:focus-visible` overrides regular `:focus` and prevents the outline from being shown even during keyboard navigation.
Marking an element that is currently hovered.
Selecting all disabled inputs.

question: Why is it a bad idea to remove the outline globally without a replacement?
options: It worsens accessibility for users who navigate with a keyboard.
Because outline cannot be disabled in CSS.
Because the browser would then stop supporting hover.
It is a problem only in Safari, because other browsers automatically add their own focus indicator even if you remove the outline.

question: What is the `::before` pseudo-element?
options: A virtual element inserted before an element’s content, typically for decorative or helper content.
A mechanism for selecting the previous sibling element, so you can style the element “before” the current one in the DOM without using `:has()`.
An alias for `:first-child`.
A way to define a fallback font.

question: What is `transition` used for?
options: Smoothly animating changes in the values of certain CSS properties between two states.
Rendering a canvas animation frame by frame.
Transitioning between routes in an SPA without JavaScript.
Only for animating `display`, because `transition` is designed primarily for switching layout modes (block/inline/flex) over time.

question: What is the difference between `transition` and `animation`?
options: A transition responds to a state change; an animation can run independently according to keyframes.
Animation is just another name for transition; the difference is only syntactic: `animation` is shorthand for `transition` with keyframes.
A transition cannot do timing functions.
An animation cannot be repeated.

question: Why is `display` typically not animated well?
options: Because it is a discrete property without smooth interpolation between values.
Because `display` can be animated only via JavaScript, since CSS cannot change `display` values over time and it must be handled by manually toggling classes frame by frame.
Because the browser cannot animate opacity.
Because `display` works only on inline elements.

question: What is `transform: translate(...)` used for in animations?
options: It allows visually moving an element, often with better performance than changing layout properties like `top/left`.
It recalculates the DOM tree.
It replaces `position: absolute`.
It changes the font size based on the viewport.

question: What is the main role of semantic HTML in styling and accessibility?
options: It provides meaningful structure for users and assistive technologies and makes more robust styling easier.
It replaces all ARIA attributes in all situations.
It is only for SEO and has no relationship to styles.
It prevents the use of flexbox.

question: Why is sufficient contrast between text and background important?
options: It improves readability and accessibility, especially for users with visual impairments or in poorer conditions.
Only so that CSS is shorter.
Contrast is important only on mobile.
The browser always automatically calculates contrast.

question: What is an advantage of the utility-first approach to styling?
options: It allows you to quickly compose a consistent look from smaller utility classes if the system is well designed.
It completely eliminates the need for a design system.
It works only without a component framework.
It prevents the use of media queries.

question: What is a disadvantage of overly deep selectors?
options: They increase coupling to the DOM structure, are harder to maintain, and complicate overriding rules.
The browser cannot process them.
They are automatically slower than inline styles by orders of magnitude in all situations.
They work only in preprocessors.

question: When does it make sense to use BEM or a similar naming convention?
options: When we want to improve the readability and predictability of CSS in larger codebases.
Only when we use purely inline styles.
Only for animations.
When we don’t want to have components.

question: What is the main benefit of CSS Modules?
options: They reduce class-name collisions by using local scoping for a specific component or file.
They remove the need for a bundler.
They allow using only grid.
They add runtime validation of stylelint rules.

question: When can inline style be inappropriate?
options: When we want pseudo-classes, media queries, better reusability, or separation of the design system from logic.
Always—inline style should never be used.
Only for text color.
Only in TypeScript projects.

question: What does `clamp()` do in CSS?
options: It lets you define a responsive value with a minimum, a preferred value, and a maximum.
It is used to hide overflowing content.
It is used only for grid columns.
It replaces `calc()` in all cases.

question: What is `calc()` used for?
options: Calculating CSS values that combine different units or expressions.
Running JavaScript in CSS.
Automatically minifying styles.
Only for calculating color contrast.

question: What is a container query?
options: A mechanism for applying styles based on a container’s size instead of the entire viewport.
A query over the browser’s style database.
An alias for a media query.
A feature available only in preprocessors.

question: Why is it good to reserve space for images or embedded content?
options: It reduces layout shifts and improves layout stability during loading.
It allows using only JPEG images.
Otherwise the browser won’t load CSS.
Only to reduce bundle size.

question: What is the benefit of design tokens for styling?
options: They introduce a shared vocabulary of values such as colors, spacing, or typography for a consistent system across the application.
They replace the need for components.
They are used only for export to Figma.
They work only with CSS-in-JS.

question: What is the main role of Next.js?
options: A framework on top of React that provides routing, rendering strategies, server capabilities, and production tooling.
A replacement for the Node.js runtime that includes its own JavaScript engine and uses an internal “Next Engine” instead of V8, so Node no longer needs to be installed.
A CSS framework for utility classes similar to Tailwind; Next.js is primarily a library of classes like `next-flex` and `next-grid` for quick component styling.
A database ORM for React apps that maps components to tables and automatically generates migrations from JSX structure.

question: What is the App Router in Next.js?
options: A modern routing system based on the directory structure in `app/` and React Server Components.
A legacy router available only in `public/` that maps static files to URLs; modern apps use it instead of `app/`.
A tool for navigating between microservices that runs in Kubernetes and decides which backend to forward a request to based on Next configuration.
An API for managing cookies in the browser without a server that replaces `document.cookie` and works even in a purely static export without any routes.

question: How is a route typically defined in Next.js App Router?
options: By adding a folder and a `page.tsx` or `page.jsx` file under the corresponding path in `app/`.
By adding an entry to `routes.json`, where URLs are manually mapped to React components and HTTP methods supported by the route are configured.
Only via a JavaScript API in `next.config.js`, because the App Router is fully configuration-based and folder structure has no effect on routes.
By generating the route via middleware, where `middleware.ts` manually parses the URL and returns the corresponding component as the response.

question: What is a layout in the App Router?
options: A shared wrapper component for a part of the route tree that can persist between navigations.
A special CSS reset file that Next automatically adds to every page; a layout is essentially where you write global styles.
A replacement for a React context provider: if you use a layout, Context is no longer needed because the layout automatically shares state to all descendants without rerenders.
Just a 404 page; a layout is a fallback shown when Next can’t find any `page.tsx` for a given path.

question: What is `loading.tsx` used for?
options: To declare loading UI for a route segment while it is loading.
To initialize a global fetch client: `loading.tsx` runs before every request and sets default headers and a base URL for `fetch` across the entire app.
To type the page props, because Next reads TypeScript types from it and enforces which parameters a route may receive.
To disable server components: if you add `loading.tsx` to a segment, Next automatically switches all components in that branch to client mode.

question: What is `error.tsx` used for?
options: To display error boundary UI for errors in the given route segment.
To log build warnings when installing dependencies; `error.tsx` is evaluated only during `next install` and prints npm errors into the UI.
To catch TypeScript errors in the editor; when TypeScript finds an error, Next “sends” it to `error.tsx` and the component renders it on the page.
To define an HTTP 500 route handler: `error.tsx` behaves like a backend endpoint that returns status 500 and JSON with error details.

question: What is a React Server Component in the context of Next.js?
options: A component rendered on the server that is not sent to the client as fully interactive JavaScript.
Every component inside `pages/`, because the Pages Router automatically server-renders all components and thus turns them into Server Components without additional rules.
A component that can run only in the browser and has unrestricted access to `window` and `document`; the “server” label means it’s loaded from the server, not that it runs there.
A component intended exclusively for route handlers, used instead of `Response.json()` and returning JSX as an HTTP response for API endpoints.

question: When do you need `use client`?
options: When a component uses client-only features like hooks, browser APIs, or event handlers.
When we want to enable SSR, because `use client` tells Next to render the page on the server and then send it to the browser as HTML.
When a component returns JSX; without `use client` Next won’t process JSX and the component must return a plain HTML string.
Only when importing a CSS module, because CSS modules are a client feature and without `use client` the styles won’t be applied.

question: What is the difference between a server component and a client component?
options: A server component runs and renders on the server; a client component hydrates and can be interactive in the browser.
A client component cannot receive props because props are sent only from the server; client components must fetch data exclusively via `localStorage` or query parameters.
A server component is always slower because the server must generate HTML on every request and can never use caching or streaming, while a client component is always fast.
A client component cannot be part of the App Router; the App Router supports only server components and for interactivity you must use the Pages Router.

question: What is the main advantage of server components?
options: They can move some work and data fetching to the server, reduce the amount of client-side JavaScript, and improve performance.
They allow using browser APIs on the server, such as `window.location` or `document.querySelector`, because server components emulate the DOM in Node.js.
They eliminate the need for API requests entirely: once you use server components, the client never has to fetch anything and all data is always automatically synchronized without HTTP.
They are only for styling: server components exist mainly so CSS can be generated on the server and only finished styles are sent to the browser.

question: What is SSR?
options: Server-side rendering, where HTML is rendered on the server per request.
Static export of all pages at build time, where SSR happens once and the resulting HTML never changes even on subsequent requests.
Rendering the page only after client hydration; SSR means the browser first downloads the JS bundle and only then the UI is rendered.
Streaming CSS to the browser: SSR stands for “Style Sheet Rendering” and means the server sends styles gradually as needed.

question: What is SSG?
options: Static Site Generation, i.e. generating HTML at build time before user requests.
Rendering only in the browser after the bundle loads; SSG means “Single Script Generation”, where everything is generated on the client by one JS script.
Server rendering on every request; SSG is a mode where the server re-renders every time so content is always up to date.
Creating a sitemap file; SSG is a tool that crawls routes and creates `sitemap.xml` so search engines can find all pages.

question: What is ISR?
options: Incremental Static Regeneration, i.e. the ability to periodically or conditionally refresh statically generated content.
A mode only for image optimization, where images are progressively “regenerated” into new formats (AVIF/WebP) on first view by a user.
A replacement for middleware: ISR stands for “Intercepted Server Requests” and is used to rewrite URLs and headers before a request reaches a route.
A mechanism for invalidating localStorage, where Next clears the browser cache on deployment and forces downloading a new bundle.

question: What is `revalidate` used for in Next.js?
options: To specify after how long cached or static content can be regenerated.
To add a route guard: `revalidate` determines after how many seconds the user’s session should be rechecked and access to the page potentially blocked.
To force client component mode; if you set `revalidate`, Next switches the page to client rendering so it can update content more often.
Only to set the session cookie, because `revalidate` stands for “refresh cookie validity” and affects cookie TTLs on the response.

question: What does `generateStaticParams` do?
options: Returns a set of dynamic parameters for pre-generating dynamic routes at build time.
Creates TypeScript types for route params and automatically exports them as `Params`, so you don’t have to manually type `params` in page components.
Is used to load env variables: Next calls it on startup and sets `process.env` for server and client based on the return value.
Works only in the `pages/` router where it generates `getStaticPaths`; in the App Router it is not used and Next ignores it.

question: What is a route handler in the App Router?
options: A server handler for HTTP methods defined by a `route.ts` file in the `app/` tree.
A React component for rendering API documentation that is automatically generated from JSDoc comments and shown under `/api`.
A build hook for Vite plugins, because route handlers are part of Next’s Vite integration and run when bundling routes.
A replacement for middleware: a route handler runs before every request and must always call `next()` similar to Express.

question: When should you use a route handler instead of a regular page?
options: When you want to handle an HTTP request and return data or another response instead of an HTML page.
When you only need to display a form; a route handler is a “form page” and is the recommended way to render inputs without React components.
When you want client-side navigation between pages; a route handler replaces `next/link` and lets you switch UI without a reload.
Only for static assets, e.g. serving images and CSS; route handlers are equivalent to a simple file server.

question: What is middleware in Next.js?
options: Code that runs before a request is completed, useful for things like redirects, auth checks, or rewrite logic.
A React hook for navigation that you call in components as `useMiddleware()` and that enables redirects on click without a router.
A utility for changing CSS on scroll; Next middleware tracks scroll position and dynamically switches classes to make sticky navigation easy.
A layer for database migrations that runs before each deployment and automatically updates the database schema according to `prisma` configuration.

question: What is `next/link` used for?
options: For client-side navigation between pages with prefetching and SPA-like behavior.
To create external URLs on the server; `next/link` is a server-only utility that signs links and adds security tokens for CSRF protection.
To fetch data from an API route: if you put an endpoint in `href`, `next/link` will call it and automatically store the response in cache for a component.
To define a canonical URL in metadata, because `<Link>` adds a `<link rel="canonical">` tag to `<head>` for SEO.

question: What is `next/navigation` used for?
options: Provides APIs for navigation and working with route state in the App Router.
Replaces `fetch` and is the recommended way to do HTTP requests in Next; `next/navigation` automatically caches and revalidates data per route.
It is intended only for the Pages Router; in the App Router navigation is done via `next/router` and `next/navigation` is only a compatibility layer.
It is used to configure webpack: it exports functions to add loaders and plugins and switches bundler config based on navigation between pages.

question: What is the benefit of `next/image`?
options: Provides image optimization, responsive sizing, and other performance benefits.
Automatically stores images in a database so they’re available offline; on the first request `next/image` downloads the image and persists it to SQLite.
Converts all images to SVG because SVG is the lightest format; during build Next rasterizes photos into vectors, reducing size.
Works only for local files without external URLs; if you use an external URL, Next rejects the image and the component won’t render.

question: Why do you need to pay attention to image width and height?
options: It helps reserve space and reduce layout shifts during loading.
Without that, Next won’t load the image at all, because `width` and `height` are required even for a regular `` and the browser otherwise blocks the request.
It matters only for PNG because with JPEG/WebP the browser always knows dimensions in advance and no layout shifts occur.
Only for correct PDF export; dimensions are mainly used in print styles and have no impact on web rendering.

question: What is `generateMetadata` used for?
options: To compute or augment page metadata, such as title, description, or Open Graph.
To create TypeScript declarations, because Next generates `.d.ts` files from metadata and speeds up typing across the whole app.
To bootstrap server actions: `generateMetadata` runs before every server action and sets headers and cache policy for it.
Only to set cookies; metadata is stored in a `__meta` cookie in Next so it can be shared between pages without the server.

question: What is a server action?
options: A server function invoked from the UI to mutate data or handle forms without having to manually create separate API endpoints for each case.
A function that always runs in the browser.
Middleware for SSR caching.
A replacement for React state.

question: When might a server action be inappropriate?
options: When you need a public API interface independent of the Next UI layer, or tighter control over backend separation.
When you are processing a form.
When you work with a database on the server.
A server action is always suitable with no exceptions.

question: What is the data fetching cache in Next.js?
options: A system that can cache fetch results depending on the render context and configuration.
A browser localStorage wrapper.
Only an internal cache for CSS modules.
A mechanism for serializing React props.

question: What does `cache: 'no-store'` mean on a fetch?
options: It tells Next that the data should not be cached and should always be fetched fresh.
It stores the response permanently in the CDN.
It disables the HTTP request and returns an empty object.
It works only in browser fetch, not on the server.

question: What does `dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` mean?
options: Forces dynamic rendering of the segment instead of static optimization.
Disables the use of Client Components.
Allows a route parameter to be optional.
Switches the build into development mode.

question: What is a hydration mismatch in Next.js?
options: A difference between the server-generated output and what the client expects during hydration.
A state where a route handler returns 404.
An error when generating a sitemap.
The result of an incorrect CSS Module import.

question: Why can using `Date.now()` or `Math.random()` directly in render cause problems?
options: It can lead to different output on the server and the client, causing a hydration mismatch.
Because these functions do not exist in Node.
Because Next renders only static JSON.
Because they disable image optimization.

question: What is the edge runtime?
options: A lighter runtime designed to run close to the user, with certain limitations compared to the full Node runtime.
A mode for GPU rendering in React.
A database replica running in the browser.
Just another name for Client Components.

question: When might the edge runtime be unsuitable?
options: When we need Node-specific APIs or libraries that the edge environment does not support.
When we want a faster response.
When we use static images.
The edge runtime is always better than the Node runtime.

question: What is `notFound()` in the App Router?
options: A helper that stops rendering of the segment with a 404 scenario.
A function for redirecting to an external URL.
An API for checking whether a file exists on disk.
A hook for getting a route parameter.

question: What does `redirect()` do?
options: Stops the current flow and returns a redirect to another path.
Rerenders only a child component without changing the URL.
It is only for route handlers.
Adds a meta refresh tag to HTML without a server.

question: What is the difference between a static and a dynamic route segment?
options: A dynamic segment contains a parameter, such as `[id]`, while a static segment has a fixed path.
A dynamic segment works only on the client.
A static segment cannot be pre-generated.

question: What is a catch-all segment like `[...slug]`?
options: A segment that captures multiple parts of the path into a single parameter.
A route handler for all HTTP methods.
A mechanism for CSS fallback.
Syntax for optional chaining in a URL.

question: What are route groups used for in the App Router?
options: To logically group segments without affecting the resulting URL structure.
To define API rate-limit groups.
To create a CSS namespace.
Only for generating a sitemap.

question: What is the main benefit of streaming rendering?

It ensures that all requests complete in parallel without errors.
It replaces the need for loading UI.

question: What is the difference between the Pages Router and the App Router?
options: The App Router is built on the newer model of segments, layouts, and Server Components, while the Pages Router uses the older `pages/` approach.
The Pages Router works only on the server and the App Router only in the browser.
The Pages Router does not support dynamic routes.
The App Router cannot do metadata.

question: When does it make sense to use `useSearchParams`?
options: When, in a Client Component, we need to read URL query parameters.
When we want to set environment variables on the server.

question: What is important when working with cookies on the server side of Next.js?
options: Be aware that accessing cookies can affect caching and dynamic rendering behavior.
Cookies can be read only in a Client Component.
Cookies are always ignored during SSR.
Next cookies are automatically stored in localStorage.

question: Why can it be inappropriate to pull an entire database client into a Client Component?

Because the database client must always be in a CSS Module.
Only because TypeScript does not support classes.

question: What is the main role of `next/font`?
options: Optimized font loading with better control over performance and layout stability.
Converting fonts into SVG icons.

A tool for generating Open Graph images.

question: What are `generateViewport` or viewport metadata used for?
options: Declaring viewport information and related metadata for a page.

Defining breakpoints for CSS.
Only for SSR caching.

question: What is a common anti-pattern in data fetching in Next.js?
options: Unnecessarily moving data fetching to the client even when it could be fetched more cheaply and securely on the server.
Using `await` in a Server Component.
Splitting a layout and a page into multiple files.
Using a route handler for API logic.

question: What is the benefit of colocating data and UI in a Server Component?
options: The component can fetch data itself close to where it’s used, without unnecessary prop drilling through multiple layers.
It means the component can no longer be tested.
It works only with Client Components.
It always requires a Redux store.

question: When should you use a Client Component even in an App Router application?
options: When you need browser interactivity, local state, event handlers, or browser-only APIs.
When the page contains text content.
When you want to reduce bundle size.
When you need a route handler.

question: What is the main trade-off of ISR?
options: It combines the speed of static content with the need to manage when and how often content is refreshed.
ISR means content will never become stale again.
ISR can be used only for Client Components.
ISR always replaces the need for a database.

question: Why is it important to understand the boundary between Server and Client Components?
options: It affects bundle size, API availability, data-fetching approach, and the security of handling secrets.
Only because of file naming.
Because otherwise CSS Modules don’t work.
It’s only about where `console.log` is rendered.

question: When does it make sense to read `searchParams` directly in a server page?
options: When we need to derive server output or data fetching from query parameters without requiring client hydration.
Only when we want to add a click handler to a button.
Only in middleware.
A server page cannot see query parameters.

question: What is the difference between a unit test and an integration test?
options: A unit test verifies a small isolated piece of logic; an integration test verifies how multiple parts work together.
A unit test always runs in a real browser and must click through the UI, while an integration test runs only on the server without a DOM and tests exclusively pure functions.
An integration test is always at least an order of magnitude slower than a unit test (e.g. exactly 10×), because that is required by the test definitions and the environment speed does not matter.

question: What is an end-to-end (e2e) test?
options: A test that simulates user behavior across the whole application, including real or realistically stubbed integration points.
A test of a single utility function without a DOM, typically verifying `formatDate()` or `sum()`; it’s called e2e because it verifies “end-to-end” math.
Only a CSS screenshot test, where only the styling output is compared without JavaScript; e2e means “effects-to-effects” and is purely visual.
