question: What does the test pyramid describe?
options: A preference for having more cheaper, lower-level tests and fewer expensive e2e tests.
A requirement to write only e2e tests, because only they are truly realistic; according to the pyramid, unit and integration tests are an anti-pattern.
A hierarchy of CSS selectors in a test, where ID selectors are at the top and class selectors at the bottom; the pyramid describes their specificity.
A set of rules for snapshots, for example that snapshots must always be stored in JSON and must be regenerated on every commit.

question: Why are flaky tests a problem?
options: They reduce trust in the test suite and make it harder to distinguish real regressions from random failures.
They mean the test covers too many edge cases, which is actually correct; a flaky test is a sign you’re testing very thoroughly.
They are beneficial for chaos engineering, because randomly failing tests simulate production incidents and thus increase system resilience.
Flaky tests are only a problem locally, not in CI, because CI always runs on the same hardware and therefore flaky behavior cannot occur.

question: What is a good property of a high-quality test?
options: It is readable, deterministic, and verifies user- or business-relevant behavior.
It contains as many `setTimeout`s as possible, because that makes the test “wait” for the UI and reduces the risk that something won’t finish; the more timeouts, the more reliable the test.
It tests the internal implementation detail of every line (including private functions), because only that ensures 100% control over the code even when changes are made.
It runs the entire stack even for a trivial pure function, because splitting tests by layers is pointless; it’s best to have only one test type that always starts the app.

question: Why is it often better to test behavior rather than implementation details?
options: The test is then more resilient to refactors as long as the system’s actual behavior does not change.
Implementation details can never be verified in JS because JavaScript does not allow function introspection or call checking; therefore only UI should be tested.
The browser cannot read the DOM, so testing implementation details is pointless; the browser works only with pixels and test frameworks cannot access the DOM.
Only because snapshot tests are forbidden; testing implementation details is allowed, but only if you don’t use snapshots.

question: What is mocking used for?
options: To replace dependencies with controlled doubles so the test is more isolated and predictable.
To automatically generate a production API from tests: during build mocks are “switched” to real implementations and backend endpoints are created.
To replace all integration tests, because if you mock everything you automatically test integration between parts of the app without needing real collaboration.
Only for testing CSS modules, because mocking is mainly used so real CSS doesn’t have to be loaded in tests and empty classes are returned instead.

question: When can excessive mocking be harmful?
options: When we end up testing the mock implementation rather than real behavior and lose confidence in how parts of the system integrate.
When we mock network calls in a unit test; that is always bad because a unit test must use the real internet otherwise it is not valid.
When we use stub data.
Mocking is never harmful; the more mocks, the better the test, because it removes randomness and tests are 100% reliable.

question: What is a stub?
options: A test double that returns pre-prepared responses without needing the full behavior of the original dependency.
A test that tracks how many times a function was called; a stub is basically a call “counter” and is used instead of a spy.
A tool for measuring coverage that “stubs” uncovered lines by marking them as covered so the coverage report looks better.
Only an HTML page snapshot.

question: What is a spy?
options: A wrapper/record around a function that lets you verify how it was called.
A replacement for an HTTP server that intercepts app requests and automatically returns responses; it’s called a spy because it “spies” on the network.
A type of CSS selector in a test.
A function for running browsers in parallel; a spy starts multiple Chrome instances at once and distributes tests across them to speed things up.

question: What are fake timers used for?
options: To control time in a test without waiting for real timeouts or intervals.
To create production performance benchmarks.
To replace the Promise API: when you enable fake timers, all Promises become synchronous and there is no need for `await` or `then`.
Only for e2e tests; fake timers cannot be used in unit/integration tests because they would break the DOM and event loop.

question: What is an accessibility query in component tests?
options: Selecting an element by role, label, or textual meaning in a way that matches how a user perceives the UI.
Selecting an element exclusively by `.class-name`.
An access method only for screen readers, not for tests; testing libraries cannot see role/label selectors and must use only IDs or classes.
A query that works only in Safari because only Safari provides a full accessibility tree API; in Chrome and Firefox these selectors do not work.

question: Why is `getByRole` often a preferred selector?
options: It tests the UI from the user’s semantic perspective and usually leads to more resilient tests.
It is always faster than all other queries, because the browser caches roles and lookup is O(1); that’s why it’s recommended mainly for test performance.
It works even without rendering the DOM; `getByRole` can select an element directly from a React component before a real DOM tree is created.
It can select any element without a role, because if an element has no role, `getByRole` automatically adds one and then selects it.

question: When can a selector using `.class` be brittle?
options: When classes change due to refactoring or styling without any change in the UI’s actual behavior.
Never—class names are the most stable contract of an application; once you have a class name it’s a public API that shouldn’t change even during a redesign.
Only when using Tailwind, because utility classes are random; with regular CSS class names are generated deterministically and therefore are always stable.
Only when the test runs in CI.

question: What is the strength of testing a form through user interactions?
options: It validates the real flow of entering values, validation, and submitting similarly to how a user does it.
It lets you avoid rendering the component because interactions can be simulated directly on a pure validation function without a DOM, which significantly speeds up the test.
It works only for uncontrolled inputs; controlled inputs cannot be filled reliably in tests because React/Vue/Angular keep the value in state.
It replaces unit testing of validation logic.

question: Why is it important to wait for asynchronous changes in the right way?
options: So the test is not dependent on race conditions and does not fail randomly based on the environment speed.
Because async code cannot be verified in tests; once something is asynchronous, a test can at most check that `setTimeout` was called, but not the result.
Only to increase coverage; waiting increases the number of executed lines and thus the reported coverage, but it has no effect on test stability.
All asynchronous changes happen synchronously after a click; `await` and waiting utilities are only for readability, not correctness.

question: What are `waitFor` or similar utilities used for?
options: To repeatedly check a condition until it passes or times out.
To create a DOM snapshot; `waitFor` saves the UI state after each render and compares it to a baseline snapshot at the end.
To mock HTTP requests; `waitFor` intercepts network calls, pauses them, and returns predefined responses so the test doesn’t need MSW or other tools.
To enable fake timers; `waitFor` automatically switches the test runner into virtual time mode and advances the clock.

question: What is a network stub in an e2e test?
options: Replacing a real network response with a controlled result to make the test more deterministic.
Production backend logic that runs in the test; a network stub is therefore the whole server, just running locally and using a real database.
An automatic retry of a failed request; stub means the test framework will repeat the call until it succeeds so tests don’t fail.
Just a browser cache response: a network stub is an enabled cache in the browser so requests are not fetched again and tests run faster.

question: When is it better to use a real backend instead of a stub?
options: When you want to validate real integration, contracts, and the behavior of the whole flow end-to-end.
Never—backends are not used in tests; e2e tests must always be 100% stubbed, otherwise determinism and reliability cannot be guaranteed.
Only for CSS visual regression, because the backend is needed only for images and fonts; for functional tests a stub is always better.
Only when testing utility functions, because they need “real data” from the database; UI tests, on the other hand, do not need the backend.

question: What is a snapshot test?
options: A test that compares the current output with a stored snapshot.
Browser performance profiling where CPU and memory are “snapshotted” and compared with a previous run to detect performance regressions.
A test that runs only against the production bundle; snapshot means the test runs only on `next build`/`vite build` output and not on source code.
Type checking of a component; a snapshot is a file with types that is compared between commits and the test fails when types change.

question: When can snapshot tests be problematic?
options: When they capture output that is too large or hard to read, and changes then get reviewed without understanding the impact.
When the snapshot contains text; text changes are forbidden in snapshots because snapshots are meant only for DOM structure without content.
Only when using TypeScript, because TS generates different snapshots than JS and type changes cause false diffs in the DOM snapshot.
A snapshot test can never provide value; it is always an anti-pattern and should be removed from the codebase without replacement.

question: What is a visual regression test?
options: A test that compares the appearance of the UI, often via screenshots or pixel diffs.
A test that validates only an API response status code; it’s called visual because it checks endpoint “health” by what the HTTP response looks like (e.g. 200/500).
A unit test for a CSS parser that verifies the browser can load and process CSS without errors; it doesn’t compare UI, only style syntax.
A database integration test that compares table schema with migrations; it’s called visual because it’s often displayed as a graph.

question: What is the benefit of accessibility testing?
options: It helps catch issues in semantics, focus, labels, or contrast before production.
It replaces all manual UX testing; if an a11y audit passes, no user testing or product review is needed anymore.
It is relevant only for government websites, because private apps don’t need to address accessibility and a11y tests are unnecessary overhead for them.
It applies only to backend APIs; accessibility is a property of correct HTTP status codes and headers, so it is tested at the server endpoint level.

question: What is the main difference between a smoke test and a regression test?
options: A smoke test quickly verifies basic system viability; a regression test more deeply covers that nothing that previously worked has been broken.
A regression test is always shorter than a smoke test, because regression tests are just a small set that runs at the beginning and the rest is skipped.
A smoke test is only for the backend; the frontend is not tested with smoke tests because the UI is too changeable and only regression E2E tests should be done.
There is no difference; smoke and regression are just two names for the same thing and differ only in when you run them in CI.

question: Why is it important to isolate tests from shared state?
options: So they don’t influence each other and their result doesn’t depend on execution order.
Only for a smaller bundle size.
Because shared state cannot be created in tests.
Isolation is necessary only for E2E tests.

question: What is a test fixture?
options: A pre-prepared set of data or an environment used repeatedly in tests.
A production database seed.
The output of a coverage report.
A CSS reset file.

question: What is the benefit of the page object pattern in E2E tests?
options: It encapsulates repeated interactions and selectors, improving test maintainability.
It replaces the need for assertions.
It works only in Selenium.
It is intended only for snapshot tests.

question: Why is it not good to rely on fixed `sleep` in E2E tests?
options: Tests are slower and more prone to flaky behavior than waiting for a real condition.
Because the browser cannot wait.
Sleep is forbidden only in CI.
Sleep always causes a memory leak.

question: What is a coverage report?
options: An overview of what part of the code was executed during tests.
Definitive proof that the application has no bugs.
A tool for mocking HTTP.
Just a list of failed tests.

question: Why isn’t high coverage alone enough?
options: Code may be executed, but without quality assertions and without covering important behavior.
Because coverage is always a harmful metric.
High coverage means there are too few tests.
Coverage cannot be measured on the frontend.

question: What is contract testing in integrations?
options: Verifying that communicating systems adhere to the expected shape and meaning of the interface.
Testing layout according to design tokens.
A snapshot of route configuration.
A unit test of CSS Modules.

question: When is it appropriate to test a custom hook or composable in isolation?
options: When it contains non-trivial logic that we want to verify outside a specific presentational component.
Never—hooks cannot be tested.
Only in an E2E test through a browser.
Only if the hook works with CSS.

question: What is a good approach to testing error-state UI?
options: Explicitly simulate an error scenario and verify what message or fallback the user will see.
Don’t test error states because they are rare.
Verify only the HTTP status without rendering UI.
Use a snapshot without assertions.

question: Why test the loading state?
options: Because users see loading, and showing/hiding it correctly is part of functional behavior.
Loading state is never rendered on the frontend.
Only for coverage.

question: What is a fake backend or mock server in tests?

A production backend running in read-only mode, used as a “safe” source of truth for tests because it cannot change data and therefore behaves deterministically.
Just a static JSON file without an HTTP layer.
A browser plugin for highlighting errors.

question: What is a test runner used for?
options: Discovering, running, and reporting test results.
Rendering the application in production, i.e., it works as a runtime “launcher” that builds the bundle and runs it in the browser on the target environment.
Defining TypeScript types.
Transforming CSS into JavaScript.

question: What is an assertion?
options: A verification of the expected test outcome.
Every component render, because the mere fact that rendering didn’t throw an exception is considered an implicit assertion of success.
Mocking a database response.
A CI pipeline structure.

question: Why can an overly broad integration test be expensive to maintain?
options: It has more dependencies, more reasons to fail, and it’s harder to find the root cause of a problem.
Because integration tests must not have assertions, so they have to be maintained via screenshots and logs, which is time-consuming.
Because the browser cannot render multiple components.
Broad integration tests are always better than smaller tests.

question: What is the role of CI in testing?
options: Automatically run tests on changes and prevent regressions before deployment.
It replaces all local development so the developer no longer runs tests locally and relies exclusively on CI to decide after pushing whether the change “passes”.
It is only for generating screenshots.

question: Why is it good to have fast tests close to the code?
options: They speed up the feedback loop and make safe refactoring easier during development.
Only for fewer files in the repository: when tests are “close” to the implementation, no additional directories are created and the build is therefore faster.
Fast tests are needed only on the backend.

question: What are test doubles generally used for?
options: To replace or observe the behavior of external dependencies in a test.
To duplicate production data into two databases so tests can compare “A/B” results and verify consistency between copies.
To ensure higher FPS in the browser.

question: What is the main advantage of explicit test data over uncontrolled randomly generated data?
options: The test intent is easier to read and failures are easier to reproduce.
Random data can never be used in tests because it breaks determinism and the test runner will automatically mark them as flaky.
Explicit data always covers more edge cases.

question: Why is it good to separate tests at the level of logic, components, and entire flows?
options: Each layer has a different cost, speed, and types of bugs it can efficiently uncover.
Because the test runner cannot run multiple test types in one project, so they must be physically split; otherwise tests would block each other in a single process.

question: What is an anti-pattern when testing a component?
options: Overly tight coupling of the test to the internal implementation structure instead of user-observable behavior.

question: What is the difference between `List<T>` and `T[]`?
options: `List<T>` is a dynamically resizable collection, while an array (`T[]`) has a fixed length.
`T[]` can grow automatically, but `List<T>` always has a fixed size.
`List<T>` is a value type, while `T[]` is a reference type.
`List<T>` cannot be iterated with `foreach`, but an array can.

question: What does `int.TryParse("123", out var x)` do?
options: Returns `true` and stores 123 in `x`.
Returns `false` and stores 123 in `x`.
Throws a `FormatException` if the input is text.
Returns `true`, but `x` will always be 0.

question: What is the purpose of the `finally` block in `try/catch/finally`?
options: Code in `finally` runs even if an exception occurs (typically for cleaning up resources).
`finally` runs only when no exception occurs.
`finally` replaces `catch` and automatically catches all exceptions.
`finally` runs only for asynchronous methods.

question: What is a `using` block/declaration for on objects implementing `IDisposable`?
options: Ensures a deterministic call to `Dispose()` when leaving the scope.
Ensures the GC collects the object immediately after the block ends.
Makes the object thread-safe.
Prevents an exception from being thrown in code inside the block.

question: What is the main difference between `class` and `struct` in C# (basic view)?
options: `class` is a reference type; `struct` is a value type.
`class` is always stored on the stack; `struct` is always stored on the heap.
`struct` can inherit from any other class; `class` cannot inherit at all.
`class` cannot implement interfaces; `struct` can.

options: null
It is undefined and depends on memory

options: private
public

question: What is the difference between `First()` and `FirstOrDefault()` in LINQ?
options: `First()` throws an exception if the sequence contains no elements; `FirstOrDefault()` returns the default value of the type.
`First()` returns the default value of the type if the sequence is empty; `FirstOrDefault()` throws an exception.
Both `First()` and `FirstOrDefault()` always return `null` when the sequence is empty, regardless of type.
`FirstOrDefault()` is always slower because it scans the whole sequence even if the first element exists.

question: Which statement about `var` in C# is correct?
options: `var` means an implicitly typed local variable; the concrete type is determined at compile time from the assigned expression.
`var` is a dynamic type and the variable’s type is determined only at runtime.
`var` can be used for fields and properties without specifying a type.
`var` causes boxing for value types because the type is not explicitly specified.

question: What is `IQueryable<T>` used for compared to `IEnumerable<T>` (typically in EF Core)?
options: `IQueryable<T>` represents a query that can be translated (e.g., to SQL) and executed later; `IEnumerable<T>` typically means iterating over data already materialized in memory.
`IQueryable<T>` is always only in-memory LINQ and can never be translated to SQL.
`IEnumerable<T>` enables deferred execution, while `IQueryable<T>` is always executed immediately.
`IQueryable<T>` is intended only for parallel queries (PLINQ) and is not used in EF Core.
